key_points: Mechanics and deal terms matter less than picking outstanding companies early
Angels must predict potential before traction is obvious
Founders’ resourcefulness is a stronger signal than most market analysis
Good deal flow comes from referrals earned by being decisive and helpful
Being a good person is part of being a good investor and improves outcomes
money_quotes: That's how you win: by investing in the right startups.
Don't get hung up on mechanics or deal terms.
To be a good angel investor, you have to be a good judge of potential.
You want to fund people who are relentlessly resourceful.
Good investors are rare, even in Silicon Valley.
posts.key_insight: Angel investing succeeds when you can spot exceptional founders and potential early, not when you optimize terms or chase hot deals.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that the long-predicted convergence of TV and computers resolved decisively in favor of computers, with TV effectively being replaced rather than merged. He attributes the shift to the Internet's open platform, Moore's Law-driven bandwidth, piracy's superior convenience, and—most powerfully—social applications that made computers essential for connection, forcing entertainment to follow. This transformation dissolves broadcast TV's dependence on synchronicity and locality, reshaping networks into service providers and opening the field to new Internet-native competitors and formats.
key_points: The Internet’s open platform accelerates innovation faster than traditional TV networks can match.
Bandwidth growth and the convenience of piracy trained audiences to watch video on computers.
Social applications created overwhelming demand for computers, pulling TV consumption onto the Internet.
Broadcast TV’s reliance on synchronicity and locality is collapsing, eroding the value of local affiliates.
Networks that cling to old distribution models will lose to more flexible, Internet-native competitors.
money_quotes: We now know the answer: computers.
This won't be convergence so much as replacement.
Facebook killed TV.
People will watch what they want when they want it, and group themselves according to whatever shared interest they feel most strongly.
That's why the Internet won.
posts.key_insight: The Internet didn’t just change how TV is delivered—it broke the broadcast model entirely by making social connection and on‑demand access the default.

posts.summary: Graham argues that the defining trait of a great startup founder is being “relentlessly resourceful,” the opposite of hapless passivity. This quality combines persistence with adaptability in the face of novel external obstacles and is more specific to startups than to creative fields like writing or painting.
key_points: The best founders are not just relentless; they are resourceful in navigating unknown obstacles.
This trait is distinct from qualities needed in other fields where obstacles are internal rather than external.
Relentless resourcefulness can often be taught or activated, especially in people conditioned by hierarchical institutions.
The limiting factor on the number of startups is the pool of people who are relentlessly resourceful.
The concept serves as both a founder selection test and a daily tactical reminder.
money_quotes: To be hapless is to be battered by circumstances — to let the world have its way with you, instead of having your way with the world.
Be relentlessly resourceful.
In any interesting domain, the difficulties will be novel.
Resourceful implies the obstacles are external, which they generally are in startups.
"Make something people want" is the destination, but "Be relentlessly resourceful" is how you get there.
posts.key_insight: Startup success hinges less on pure persistence and more on adaptive, relentless resourcefulness in the face of novel external obstacles.

posts.summary: Paul Graham answers Inc's question by choosing the five founders who most influenced his own thinking about startups. He frames “interesting” as practical influence—people he cites when advising companies—and highlights each founder’s distinctive contribution, from design sensibility to ruthless clarity to talent-first execution. The list doubles as a compact set of startup lessons about taste, talent, focus, and force of will.
key_points: Influence on his own advice is his test for “most interesting.”
Steve Jobs exemplifies enduring influence through design and taste.
TJ Rodgers models brutally candid, pragmatic, idea-pruning thinking.
Larry & Sergey proved a talent-first, hacker-heavy approach can win early.
Paul Buchheit’s focus on making a few users love you is a core startup lesson.
money_quotes: A lot of startup culture is Apple culture.
What makes him unique is his sense of design.
But Google pushed this idea further than anyone had before.
All you need is good hackers.
it's better, initially, to make a small number of users really love you than a large number kind of like you.
posts.key_insight: The founders who matter most are those whose distinctive ways of thinking—about design, talent, focus, and ambition—become reusable lessons for building startups.

posts.summary: Graham argues that the most impactful, low-cost way for the U.S. government to boost startups is to create a new visa specifically for founders. He points to the high share of immigrant founders and suggests policy openness makes this a timely proposal.
key_points: Create a founder visa to increase the number of startups
Policy would cost the government nothing
Immigrants already constitute a large share of VC-backed founders
Current administration appears open to suggestions, making this a good moment
money_quotes: I usually avoid politics, but since we now seem to have an administration that's open to suggestions, I'm going to risk making one.
The single biggest thing the government could do to increase the number of startups in this country is a policy that would cost nothing: establish a new class of visa for
About Half of VC-Backed Company Founders are Immigrants
posts.key_insight: A no-cost founder visa could meaningfully increase U.S. startup formation by attracting immigrant entrepreneurs.

posts.summary: The excerpt argues that startups might represent a new economic phase comparable to the Industrial Revolution, though the author is uncertain and only suggests a reasonable chance. The post hints at a second idea that would “explode” when combined with the first, but the provided text cuts off before it is revealed.
key_points: The author suspects startups could mark a new economic phase on the scale of the Industrial Revolution.
He is unsure of this claim but thinks it is plausibly true.
The essay sets up a second, unrevealed idea meant to combine with the first for a larger conclusion, but the excerpt ends prematurely.
money_quotes: Recently I realized I'd been holding two ideas in my head that would explode if combined.
The first is that startups may represent a new economic phase, on the scale of the Industrial Revolution.
I'm not sure of this, but there seems a decent chance it's true.
posts.key_insight: The author tentatively frames startups as a potentially epochal economic shift, while withholding a second idea that would sharpen the argument.

posts.summary: Graham argues Twitter matters because it functions as a new messaging protocol where you don't specify recipients, and successful new protocols are rare. Its private ownership makes it even more unusual, yet its slow monetization helped it feel like an open protocol, which aided adoption.
key_points: Twitter is best understood as a new messaging protocol rather than a mere product.
Successful new protocols are rare and therefore significant.
Twitter’s private ownership is unusually uncommon for a protocol.
Minimal early monetization helped Twitter feel open and spread faster.
money_quotes: The reason is that it's a new messaging protocol, where you don't specify the recipients.
New protocols are rare. Or more precisely, new protocols that take off are.
But Twitter is a protocol owned by a private company. That's even rarer.
Because they haven't tried to control it too much, Twitter feels to everyone like previous protocols.
That must have made it easier for Twitter to spread.
posts.key_insight: Twitter’s significance comes from being a rare, widely adopted protocol whose openness-feeling—despite private ownership—enabled rapid spread.

posts.summary: Graham argues that makers (programmers, writers) work best in large, uninterrupted blocks, while managers operate in hour-sized slots. Meetings that are trivial on a manager’s schedule are costly on a maker’s schedule, fragmenting days and lowering ambition. He suggests clustering meetings (office hours) and increasing awareness so managers respect the real cost to makers.
key_points: Maker work requires long, uninterrupted time blocks; hour slices are too short to start and finish hard work.
Meetings fragment a maker’s day and can reduce morale enough to derail ambitious projects.
Managers can default to their schedule because they hold power, but should restrain meeting demands on makers.
Office hours and deliberate batching can reconcile the two schedules without constant interruption.
Speculative meetings are cheap for managers but expensive for makers, creating social friction.
money_quotes: There are two types of schedule, which I'll call the manager's schedule and the maker's schedule.
You can't write or program well in units of an hour.
When you're operating on the maker's schedule, meetings are a disaster.
For someone on the maker's schedule, having a meeting is like throwing an exception.
Those of us on the maker's schedule are willing to compromise.
posts.key_insight: Meetings feel cheap to managers but are disproportionately expensive to makers, so scheduling should respect the need for long, uninterrupted blocks of creative work.

posts.summary: Paul Graham defines “ramen profitable” as a startup earning just enough to cover founders’ living expenses, a threshold that buys time and removes the urgent need to raise money. He argues this state improves leverage with investors, validates demand, boosts morale, and reduces the distraction of fundraising, while warning it can tempt founders into low-growth consulting instead of building a scalable product.
key_points: Ramen profitability is a new, low-cost form of profitability enabled by cheap software startups.
It shifts power from investors to founders by removing the need to raise money immediately.
Early profitability signals real demand, focus on user needs, and disciplined spending.
The morale impact is large because survival becomes the default rather than failure.
The main risk is drifting into consulting instead of building a scalable product.
money_quotes: Ramen profitable means a startup makes just enough to pay the founders' living expenses.
The main significance of this type of profitability is that you're no longer at the mercy of investors.
A startup that reaches ramen profitability may be more likely to succeed than not.
Raising money is terribly distracting.
Ramen profitability is not the destination.  A startup's destination is to grow really big; ramen profitability is a trick for not dying en route.
posts.key_insight: Hitting ramen profitability gives founders time and leverage to focus on building a scalable product without the immediate pressure of fundraising.

posts.summary: Graham argues the Segway failed because it makes riders look smug, which triggers social backlash, regardless of the device's technical merits. He suggests a redesign that appears more effortful and culturally aligned (like skateboards or bikes). He also critiques Segway the company for raising too much money too easily, which insulated it from real-world feedback that would have revealed the problem.
key_points: Public perception, not engineering, is a core obstacle to Segway adoption.
Riders look smug because the device appears effortless, inviting ridicule.
A more effort-looking form factor could have improved market acceptance.
Segway’s fundraising success let it avoid iterative learning from real users.
Lack of unfiltered feedback led the company down a blind alley.
money_quotes: The Segway hasn't delivered on its initial promise, to put it mildly.
Someone riding a Segway looks like a dork.
The reason you look like a dork riding a Segway is that you look smug.
So there may be a way to capture more of the market Segway hoped to reach: make a version that doesn't look so easy for the rider.
If they'd had to grow the company gradually, by iterating through several versions they sold to real users, they'd have learned pretty quickly that people looked stupid riding them.
posts.key_insight: Segway’s failure was less about technology and more about social optics, a lesson the company missed because easy funding insulated it from real-world feedback.

posts.summary: Paul Graham relays architect Kate Courteau's observations after immersion in Y Combinator, highlighting how startup culture diverges from the "normal" world. Her list emphasizes high failure rates, rapid pivots, low startup costs, scrappy founders, dense tech networks, consistent advice, casual successful founders, the importance of mentorship, and the solitary nature of startup work. Graham concludes that startup culture may be a leading indicator of broader future norms.
key_points: Startups live under constant risk of failure, even famous ones.
Ideas frequently change; fast launch and iteration are core practices.
Founders are scrappy, operate with little money, and often work in solitude.
Silicon Valley is unusually tech-saturated, with strong advice networks.
Successful founders appear casual and unassuming compared to VCs.
money_quotes: Starting a startup is like science in that you have to follow the truth wherever it leads.
You can barely renovate a bathroom for the cost of starting a startup.
If you're not threatening, you're probably not doing anything new, and dignity is merely a sort of plaque.
"You could do it in a box."
So startup culture may not merely be different in the way you'd expect any subculture to be, but a leading indicator.
posts.key_insight: Startup culture is defined by rapid iteration, scrappy founders, and constant uncertainty, and may foreshadow how work and innovation will evolve more broadly.

posts.summary: Graham argues that determination is the strongest predictor of startup success, more important than raw intelligence. He breaks determination into willfulness balanced by discipline and directed by ambition, noting that discipline and ambition can be cultivated. He adds that love of the work reduces the need for determination, but creating wealth often means serving others’ needs and using determination to bridge the gap.
key_points: Determination outweighs intelligence as a predictor of startup success.
Determination is a balance of willfulness and discipline, guided by ambition.
Discipline and ambition are malleable and can be increased.
Temptation grows with success, so determination can erode without increased discipline.
Loving the work reduces reliance on determination, but wealth often requires serving others’ needs.
money_quotes: We learned quickly that the most important predictor of success is determination.

In most domains, talent is overrated compared to determination—partly because it makes a better story, partly because it gives onlookers an excuse for being lazy, and partly because after a while determination starts to look like talent.
Determination implies your willfulness is balanced by discipline.
If determination is effectively the product of will and discipline, then you can become more determined by being more disciplined.
So here in sum is how determination seems to work: it consists of willfulness balanced with discipline, aimed by ambition.
posts.key_insight: Startup success hinges more on cultivated determination—willfulness balanced by discipline and aimed by ambition—than on raw intelligence.

posts.summary: Graham argues that most essays are written to persuade, which leads writers to add ingratiating spin that can distort the ideas themselves. He prefers writing to discover surprising truths, even if that means offending readers, because clarity and concision help him see what the ideas really are.
key_points: Writing to persuade encourages padding and spin that can blur the underlying idea.
Concision helps reveal the real argument; extra politeness can become self-deception.
Writing to discover targets an unbiased reader, not the actual audience’s misconceptions.
The more surprising the conclusion, the more persuasive drag slows honest inquiry.
money_quotes: Most writers write to persuade, if only out of habit or politeness. But I don't write to persuade; I write to figure out.
If you want to please people who are mistaken, you can't simply tell the truth.
You don't know what the ideas are until you get them down to the fewest words.
Writing to persuade and writing to discover are diametrically opposed.
The danger of the second paragraph is not merely that it's longer. It's that you start to lie to yourself.
posts.key_insight: To discover truth in writing, you must resist persuasive padding and cut down to the fewest words, even if that risks offending readers.

He warns that beginners should learn the form openly, yet also learn to escape it to write dynamic essays.",

posts.summary: Graham argues that publishers were never really selling content; they were selling the medium, and pricing was driven by format rather than quality. As the medium evaporates, selling copies becomes untenable, so publishers must either give content away and monetize indirectly or embody it in experiences or physical objects. He sees new winners arising from products that use technology to deliver something people couldn’t have before, not from attempts to preserve old revenue streams.
key_points: Pricing in publishing has historically been tied to format, not the value of the content itself.
Consumers rarely pay for information unless it helps them make money.
Digital distribution shifts power to channel owners and drives prices down.
Most media will need indirect monetization (ads, concerts, experiences) or physical embodiment.
The best opportunities come from new forms that leverage technology, not defensive tactics.
money_quotes: In fact consumers never really were paying for content, and publishers weren't really selling it either.
Economically, the print media are in the business of marking up paper.
People will pay for information they think they can make money from.
Once a toll becomes painful, people start to find ways around it, and that's pretty easy with digital content.
When you see something that's taking advantage of new technology to give people something they want that they couldn't have before, you're probably looking at a winner.
posts.key_insight: Publishing can’t rely on selling copies anymore; sustainable models must either monetize attention or create new forms and experiences enabled by technology.

posts.summary: Paul Graham synthesizes feedback from YC founders to show that startups are far more extreme than people expect, mainly because they unconsciously model them as jobs. The surprises cluster around intense cofounder dynamics, total life immersion, emotional volatility, and the need for persistence, fast iteration, and user-driven pivots. The core argument is that startups are not just hard—they are a different mode of life and work, and founders must internalize that difference to survive and succeed.
key_points: Cofounder character and relationship quality matter more than raw ability, and the relationship must be actively maintained.
Startups consume your life and produce extreme emotional swings; persistence is the central differentiator.
Everything takes longer than expected, so long-term thinking and patience reduce stress and improve decisions.
Success comes from many small, iterative actions, launching minimal products, and engaging users early.
Execution beats ideas; competitors are often less threatening than expected, while luck and investor psychology play outsized roles.
money_quotes: You haven't seen someone's true colors unless you've worked with them on a startup.
In a startup, things seem great one moment and hopeless the next. And by next, I mean a couple hours later.
I've been surprised again and again by just how much more important persistence is than raw intelligence.
If your first version is so impressive that trolls don't make fun of it, you waited too long to launch.
Starting a startup was like I said, but way more so.
posts.key_insight: Most startup shocks come from assuming it will feel like a job, but startups are a far more intense, uncertain, and all-consuming way of working that rewards persistence, iteration, and adaptability.

posts.summary: Graham argues that Apple’s App Store approval process misunderstands how software is built, turning a fast-iterate discipline into slow, arbitrary gatekeeping. This damages Apple’s reputation with programmers, degrades app quality, and risks long-term platform health by alienating the very developers who make platforms succeed.
key_points: Apple applies a music/iTunes intermediary model to software, which doesn’t work for apps.
Approval delays break the launch-fast-and-iterate cycle and leave users with stale, buggy versions.
Programmer goodwill is a strategic asset; losing it harms hiring and platform vitality.
Developers build for the platforms they personally use, keeping iPhone apps plentiful despite mistreatment.
A credible alternative would need to win programmers by offering capabilities Apple’s lock-down forbids.
money_quotes: The App Store is an ongoing karma leak.
Software isn't like music or books.
By obstructing that process, Apple is making them do bad work, and programmers hate that as much as Apple would.
They make such great stuff, but they're such assholes.
When you sell a platform, developers make or break you.
posts.key_insight: Apple’s strict App Store gatekeeping undermines software’s iterative nature and erodes the developer goodwill that ultimately sustains a platform.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that the best startup ideas grow organically from problems you personally experience, rather than being invented for hypothetical users. Young founders, in particular, should focus on fixing broken things in their own lives because it’s hard to predict others’ needs without experience. If you follow real, fixable problems—even ones that seem like “toys”—you’re likely to stumble into something valuable that can become a company.
key_points: Two types of ideas exist: organic (from your own needs) and made-up (for others), with the most successful startups skewing organic.
Young founders are poor at predicting others’ needs but excellent at spotting fixable brokenness in new technology.
Organic ideas often look small or unserious at first; that’s a positive signal, not a red flag.
Focus on fixing what feels broken rather than on starting a company, and value will compound into a startup.
The most valuable opportunities are unmet needs that are just becoming technically fixable.
money_quotes: The best way to come up with startup ideas is to ask yourself the question: what do you wish someone would make for you?
Organic startup ideas usually don't seem like startup ideas at first.
So if you want to come up with organic startup ideas, I'd encourage you to focus more on the idea part and less on the startup part.
Don't be discouraged if what you produce initially is something other people dismiss as a toy.  In fact, that's a good sign.
There's nothing more valuable than an unmet need that is just becoming fixable.
posts.key_insight: The strongest startups begin by fixing a real problem you personally feel, even if it seems small or toy-like at first.

posts.summary: Graham argues that most addictions are concentrated, technologically improved versions of milder predecessors, and that this concentration is accelerating. The same progress that yields benefits also makes more things intensely engaging, pushing people to live increasingly “abnormal” lives if they want to function well. Because social customs adapt slowly, individuals will have to proactively decide what to avoid and set personal boundaries.
key_points: Technological progress concentrates what we want, and that concentration often turns pleasures into addictions.
The pace of new, more addictive products is accelerating, outstripping the slow evolution of social norms.
As addictiveness rises, statistical normality diverges from what is healthy or productive.
Customs and laws can mitigate addiction, but they lag and have costs, so personal strategies become essential.
Avoidance will increasingly define people who want to stay effective amid pervasive, evolving temptations.
money_quotes: The world is more addictive than it was 40 years ago.
You can probably take it as a rule of thumb from now on that if people don't think you're weird, you're living badly.
Unless we want to be canaries in the coal mine of each new addiction—the people whose sad example becomes a lesson to future generations—we'll have to figure out for ourselves what to avoid and how.
I've avoided most addictions, but the Internet got me because it became addictive while I was using it.
We'll increasingly be defined by what we say no to.
posts.key_insight: As technology rapidly intensifies what we like, social norms cannot keep up, so living well will increasingly require deliberate, sometimes eccentric, personal limits.

posts.summary: Graham argues that most fortunes are lost not by lavish spending but by bad investments that bypass our self-indulgence alarms. He extends the analogy to time: the biggest waste isn’t leisure but “fake work” that feels productive while yielding little. The fix is to develop new, learned alarms for these subtler traps.
key_points: Bad investments, not luxury spending, are the most common way fortunes disappear.
Self-indulgence alarms are strong for spending but weak for investing and need to be retrained.
Time is lost most dangerously through fake work that mimics real effort.
Avoiding pleasure is no longer sufficient protection in a more complex world.
money_quotes: But in fact the way most fortunes are lost is not through excessive expenditure, but through bad investments.
The solution is to develop new alarms.
The most dangerous way to lose time is not to spend it having fun, but to spend it doing fake work.
With time, as with money, avoiding pleasure is no longer enough to protect you.
The most dangerous traps now are new behaviors that bypass our alarms about self-indulgence by mimicking more virtuous types.
posts.key_insight: The biggest losses of money and time come from activities that feel responsible—bad investments and fake work—so you need learned alarms, not just guilt about indulgence.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that your best work depends on what your mind naturally drifts to during “ambient” moments, like in the shower. Because that top idea gets the most unconscious processing, letting the wrong thing dominate it—especially fundraising or disputes—starves more important work of attention. The practical takeaway is to shape your situations so the most urgent problems are the ones you want your mind to keep returning to.
key_points: Ambient, unforced thinking is crucial for solving hard problems, and it mainly benefits whatever your mind treats as the top idea.
Raising money and disputes are attention sinks that crowd out better work, slowing startups and individuals alike.
You can’t directly control drifting thoughts, but you can control situations so the right problems become top-of-mind.
Ignoring injuries and disputes is not just virtuous but protective of your attention.
A simple diagnostic is to notice what your thoughts return to in the shower; if it’s wrong, change something.
money_quotes: It’s hard to do a really good job on anything you don’t think about in the shower.
I think most people have one top idea in their mind at any given time.
Which means it’s a disaster to let the wrong idea become the top one in your mind.
Money matters are particularly likely to become the top idea in your mind.
Someone who does you an injury hurts you twice: first by the injury itself, and second by taking up your time afterward thinking about it.
posts.key_insight: Shape your commitments so your mind’s default drift serves the work you value most, because whatever sits at the top of your thoughts will quietly determine your progress.

posts.summary: Graham argues that startup funding is shifting toward smaller, founder-controlled rounds as VCs face pressure from super-angels and changing startup capital needs. He predicts faster, simpler deals, less investor control (board seats, option pool requirements, traction-gating), and more founder power overall. This shift should ultimately benefit both founders and investors by increasing startup formation and improving outcomes.
key_points: Angel and super-angel investing is expanding to meet the demand for smaller raises.
Founder-controlled rounds will replace investor-controlled rounds, with rolling closes and standardized terms.
Investors who rely on herd behavior or traction-gating will be disadvantaged.
VCs must adapt by doing more, smaller deals and taking fewer board seats or drift into later-stage investing.
More founder power can reduce investor overcontrol and increase the total number of startups.
money_quotes: The future of angel rounds looks more like this: instead of a fixed round size, startups will do a rolling close, where they take money from investors one at a time till they feel they have enough.
Founders are becoming increasingly powerful relative to investors.
One by one, all the things founders dislike about raising money are going to get eliminated.
Investors may actually make more money as a result.
Our main competitor is employers.
posts.key_insight: The balance of power in startup funding is shifting toward founders, leading to faster, simpler, and more founder-friendly deals that can expand the overall startup ecosystem.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that Yahoo’s decline stemmed from two early, compounding flaws: dependence on easy money from banner ads and startup ad spending, and an identity crisis that made it resist being a true technology company. These forces distracted leadership from the long-term value of search and undermined a hacker-centric culture, leading to mediocre software and a talent death spiral.
key_points: Yahoo’s banner-ad windfall and startup ad spending masked the real value of search and delayed strategic focus.
Yahoo’s self-image as a media company led to suit-centric management and undervaluing engineering.
Treating programming as a commodity produced weaker software and drove away top talent.
Hacker-centric culture is essential for any company that needs great software.
money_quotes: Yahoo had two problems Google didn't: easy money, and ambivalence about being a technology company.
As long as customers were writing big checks for banner ads, it was hard to take search seriously.
The worst consequence of trying to be a media company was that they didn't take programming seriously enough.
In technology, once you have bad programmers, you're doomed.
In the software business, you can't afford not to have a hacker-centric culture.
posts.key_insight: Easy money and a suit-centric identity can blind a company to the need for a hacker-centric culture, and once technical excellence erodes, recovery is unlikely.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that convertible notes enable "high-resolution" fundraising by letting startups offer different effective prices to different investors, which breaks the deadlocks caused by investors waiting on one another. He also contends fixed-size equity rounds are a poor fit for angel investing because startups can’t know the optimal round size in advance, and that markets should evolve toward flexible pricing across investors.
key_points: Convertible notes speed angel rounds by allowing varied effective valuations and rewarding early, risk-taking investors.
Investor herd behavior creates deadlocks; flexible terms help startups move first movers forward.
Different investors add different value and should receive different terms, not uniform pricing.
Fixed-size equity rounds force premature decisions on amount raised and often mimic VC rounds inappropriately.
High-resolution fundraising will favor bold investors and may shut out cautious ones from top deals.
money_quotes: By far the biggest influence on investors' opinions of a startup is the opinion of other investors.
Convertible notes let startups beat such deadlocks by rewarding investors willing to move first with lower (effective) valuations.
Markets always evolve toward higher resolution.
It's just not reasonable to expect startups to pick an optimal round size in advance, because that depends on the reactions of investors, and those are impossible to predict.
Whereas the "who else is investing?" type of investors will not only pay higher prices, but may not be able to get into the best deals at all.
posts.key_insight: Flexible, investor-specific pricing in angel rounds breaks herd-driven deadlocks and better aligns fundraising terms with real risk and value.

posts.summary: Graham argues that the most important qualities in startup founders are determination and flexibility, because startups are full of obstacles and unpredictable pivots. He adds that imagination, a mild rule-breaking streak, and strong co-founder friendship separate the best founders from merely smart ones.
key_points: Determination matters more than raw intelligence once a baseline is met.
Founders must pair determination with flexibility to adapt quickly.
Imagination produces ideas that look initially bad but are on the right side of crazy.
A bit of naughtiness helps founders break non-essential rules to win.
Strong, trusting co-founder relationships are crucial for resilience.
money_quotes: But as long as you're over a certain threshold of intelligence, what matters most is determination.
The world of startups is so unpredictable that you need to be able to modify your dreams on the fly.
In the startup world, most good ideas seem bad initially.
They delight in breaking rules, but not rules that matter.
Startups do to the relationship between the founders what a dog does to a sock: if it can be pulled apart, it will be.
posts.key_insight: The best founders combine relentless determination with adaptive flexibility, imaginative thinking, a rule-bending edge, and strong co-founder bonds.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that Silicon Valley isn’t defined by grand landmarks but by subtle, lived-in places where startups, investors, and ideas cluster. He maps a set of locations—from Stanford and University Ave to Sand Hill Road and Google—showing how geography, culture, and everyday routines create the Valley’s distinctive startup ecosystem.
key_points: Silicon Valley’s character is revealed in understated places rather than monuments.
Stanford’s setting and proximity to San Francisco helped catalyze the Valley’s growth.
Cafes and walkable streets enable constant founder–investor interactions and idea exchange.
The Valley has shifted south as costs rise, changing where startups congregate.
The physical landscape (mountains, coast, highways) shapes the region’s feel and culture.
money_quotes: At first glance it doesn't seem there's anything to see.
A surprising amount of the work of the Valley is done in the cafes on or just off University Ave in Palo Alto.

It's a smart move to put a startup in a place with restaurants and people walking around instead of in an office park, because then the people who work there want to stay there, instead of fleeing as soon as conventional working hours end.
Visiting Sand Hill Road reminds you that the opposite of "down and dirty" would be "up and clean."
You sense there is something afoot.
posts.key_insight: Silicon Valley’s power comes less from iconic landmarks than from a dense web of walkable, social, idea-rich places that make startup culture possible.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that startup funding has shifted from a two-player world (angels and VCs) to a three-player market with super-angels, creating real competition and changing deal structure. This competition is raising valuations, speeding rounds, and giving founders more leverage, though it also pressures VCs and introduces new dynamics like signaling risk.
key_points: Super-angels emerged to fill the mid-sized funding gap and now compete with both angels and VCs.
Competition between super-angels and VCs drives faster rounds, higher valuations, and less dilution for founders.
VCs are threatened but may retain advantages in brand and hands-on support for select winners.
Medium-sized angel rounds let founders preserve control and reduce fundraising stress compared to traditional Series A.
Signaling risk from VC participation exists but is often overstated if a startup shows strong results.
money_quotes: After barely changing at all for decades, the startup funding business is now in what could, at least by comparison, be called turmoil.
The arrival of a new type of investor is big news for startups, because there used to be only two and they rarely competed with one another.
Till now, VCs' claims about how much value they added were sort of like the government's.
Rate of return is what matters in investing—not the multiple you get, but the multiple per year.
As a rule, the only thing that can kill a good startup is the startup itself.
posts.key_insight: The rise of super-angels turns startup funding into a competitive market that improves terms for founders while reshaping how and why VCs invest.

posts.summary: Graham argues that founders retaining board control after a Series A is no longer rare and is rapidly becoming the norm. He claims this shift is driven by top startups setting the standard and by VCs realizing they can still influence without needing to compel, ultimately leading to better founder selection.
key_points: Founder board control after Series A is increasingly common, especially among YC-backed startups.
Board control matters in practice because it shapes discussions and outcomes even when votes are rare.
The best startups are more likely to retain control, and they set trends for the rest of the market.
VCs often resist founder control for signaling reasons, but the resistance weakens once it's no longer seen as a concession.
VCs may make better choices when they know they cannot easily replace founders.
money_quotes: Founders retaining control after a series A is clearly heard-of.
Control of a company is a more complicated matter than simply outvoting other parties in board meetings.
So while board control is not total control, it's not imaginary either.
VCs will still be able to convince; they just won't be able to compel.
If they know they can't fire the founders, they'll have to choose founders they can trust.
posts.key_insight: Founder board control after Series A is quickly becoming standard, benefiting both startups and VCs by aligning power with trust and performance.

posts.summary: Graham argues that iPhones, iPads, and similar devices are best understood as tablets, and that their real significance is enabling a wave of “ephemeralization” where physical objects are replaced by software. This shift will make many specialized devices unnecessary, creating big opportunities for startups and platform competition beyond Apple.
key_points: The term “tablet” better captures the new class of devices than “mobile devices.”
Tablets enable software to replace many specialized physical objects.
This creates near-term startup opportunities by spotting things tablets can make unnecessary.
Ephemeralization is a powerful historical force driving tablet adoption.
Platform openness and developer access will determine competition with Apple.
money_quotes: The iPhone isn't so much a phone as a replacement for a phone.
Many if not most of the special-purpose objects around us are going to be replaced by apps running on tablets.
The advantages of doing things in software on a single device are so great that everything that can get turned into software will.
The iPhone and the iPad have effectively drilled a hole that will allow ephemeralization to flow into a lot of new areas.
Give hackers an inch and they'll take you a mile.
posts.key_insight: Tablets matter less as devices than as a platform for software to replace physical objects, unlocking a broad wave of opportunity and competition.

posts.summary: Paul Graham presents an internal email exchange showing how investors debated Airbnb’s prospects and how he tried to persuade Fred Wilson to invest. The thread illustrates how early-stage decisions hinge on founder quality, user insight, and the ability to imagine a startup evolving beyond its initial niche.
key_points: Investor hesitation is common even for breakout startups, and public admissions of misses are rare.
Early-stage conviction often comes from founder quality and on-the-ground user understanding, not just the initial idea.
Big startups frequently start in narrow niches and expand; imagining that evolution is crucial to evaluating them.
Marketplace businesses can grow along a continuum from small, informal supply to professional, large-scale providers.
YC actively advocates for portfolio companies behind the scenes to help investors see their potential.
money_quotes: Ideas can morph.
Practically every really big startup could say, five years later, "believe it or not, we started out doing ___."
It just seemed a very good sign to me that these guys were actually on the ground in NYC hunting down (and understanding) their users.
Once something becomes a big marketplace, you ignore it at your peril.
There's no reason this couldn't be as big as Ebay.
posts.key_insight: Evaluating early-stage startups requires seeing past the initial niche to the founders’ ability to grow a marketplace into something much bigger.

posts.summary: Graham argues that while patent reform is slow through government, the industry can reduce harmful patent abuse by adopting a voluntary “patent pledge.” The pledge publicly commits companies not to use software patents offensively against small startups, making bad actors conspicuous and less attractive to employees and investors. This downstream pressure targets innovation-suppressing behavior without waiting for legislation.
key_points: Patent reform via government is slow, but companies can curb abusive patent use through public pledges.
The pledge aims to shame and isolate firms that use patents to suppress small competitors.
Voluntary standards can attract better talent and reinforce ethical competition.
money_quotes: As well as pinching off the stream of patents at the point where they're issued, we may in some cases be able to pinch it off at the point where they're used.
One way of using patents that clearly does not encourage innovation is when established companies with bad products use patents to suppress small competitors with good products.
The way to do it is to get the companies that are above pulling this sort of trick to pledge publicly not to.
Technology companies win by attracting the most productive people, and the most productive people are attracted to employers who hold themselves to a higher standard than the law requires.
Companies that use patents on startups are attacking innovation at the root.
posts.key_insight: A simple public pledge can shift incentives and curb the most innovation‑damaging uses of software patents without waiting for government reform.

posts.summary: Graham argues that most startups fail by default, and the reason hubs like Silicon Valley outperform is that they provide an antidote: a culture where starting startups is normal and a dense network of chance meetings that deliver critical help. Both advantages come from having many startup people in one place, which sets norms, increases serendipity, and supplies the specific expertise any given startup needs. The hub’s real asset is people, not infrastructure, and the atmosphere it creates is notably optimistic.
key_points: Startup failure is the default; hubs raise survival odds by supplying the antidote.
Two hub advantages are social normalization of startups and high-value chance meetings.
Density matters because it fuels serendipity, supplies diverse help, and sets ambitious norms.
The key asset of a hub is people and their willingness to help, not physical infrastructure.
Optimism is a distinguishing ambient signal of a thriving startup hub.
money_quotes: The problem is not that most towns kill startups. It's that death is the default for startups, and most towns don't save them.
The antidote is people.
Chance meetings produce miracles to compensate for the disasters that characteristically befall startups.
In most places the atmosphere pulls you back toward the mean.
The word that came to mind was optimism.
posts.key_insight: Startup hubs work because dense communities make startups feel normal and create frequent, high-leverage chance encounters that can rescue fragile companies.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that many great startup ideas go unseen because founders have “schlep blindness” — an unconscious aversion to tedious, intimidating work. Startups require handling unpleasant tasks, and the scariest ideas are often the most valuable because others avoid them. He suggests overcoming this by recognizing that schleps are inevitable and by asking what problems you wish someone else would solve for you.
key_points: Schlep blindness is an unconscious tendency to avoid ideas that involve painful, tedious work.
Startups are defined by the schleps they take on; writing code alone is not enough.
Ambitious, scary ideas face less competition and can be undervalued opportunities.
Ignorance of the full scope of obstacles can help founders start and grow into the problems.
A useful prompt is to ask what problem you wish someone else would solve for you.
money_quotes: A company is defined by the schleps it will undertake.
And schleps should be dealt with the same way you'd deal with a cold swimming pool: just jump in.
That's schlep blindness.
That scariness makes ambitious ideas doubly valuable.
Instead of asking "what problem should I solve?" ask "what problem do I wish someone else would solve for me?"
posts.key_insight: The best startup opportunities often hide behind intimidating, tedious work, and recognizing — then embracing — those schleps is a competitive advantage.

posts.summary: Paul Graham reflects on a June 1998 snapshot of Viaweb’s site to show how different the web, tooling, and growth channels were before Yahoo acquired the company. He uses specific anecdotes about design constraints, infrastructure, marketing, and product decisions to illustrate the scrappy realities of early web startups and how they adapted to the era’s limitations.
key_points: The 1998 web imposed severe constraints on page size, fonts, and rendering, shaping Viaweb’s design choices.
Marketing depended heavily on press coverage; online discovery channels like modern SEO and Google did not yet exist.
Viaweb relied on in-office servers and limited bandwidth, reflecting the absence of cloud infrastructure.
Product and ops details (e.g., Cybercash, test drives, RTML) show how teams improvised to meet expectations and user needs.
money_quotes: The first thing one notices is is how tiny the pages are.
Back in the 90s, to get users you had to get mentioned in magazines and newspapers.
Notice anything missing?  Google was incorporated that September.
In those days there was no AWS.
The whole Viaweb site was made with our software, even though it wasn't an online store, because we wanted to experience what our users did.
posts.key_insight: Early web startups succeeded by improvising around severe technical and marketing constraints that feel foreign today.

posts.summary: Graham argues that the strongest startups are led by founders who are deeply resourceful, especially in conversation, able to take a hint and chase down all the implications without hand-holding. The weakest founders appear “hard to talk to,” not because they lack intelligence but because they resist uncomfortable implications, which signals a deeper lack of resourcefulness that hurts fundraising, user acquisition, and idea generation.
key_points: The best founders are “fire-and-forget,” needing only a lead to close funding or other opportunities.
Being hard to talk to is a visible symptom of low resourcefulness, not the direct cause of failure.
Conversational resourcefulness means following implications, even inconvenient ones, rather than filtering advice through denial.
Successful teams evaluate feedback with fresh eyes and logical reasons, not pre-decided rationalizations.
money_quotes: The startups that do best are fire-and-forget in the sense that all you have to do is give them a lead, and they'll close it, whatever type of lead it is.
What it means is that if someone is wise, all you have to do is say one word to them, and they'll understand immediately.
Understanding all the implications — even the inconvenient implications — of what someone tells you is a subset of resourcefulness.
The unsuccessful founders weren't stupid.  Intellectually they were as capable as the successful founders of following all the implications of what one said to them.  They just weren't eager to.
So being hard to talk to was not what was killing the unsuccessful startups.  It was a sign of an underlying lack of resourcefulness.
posts.key_insight: Conversational resourcefulness—eagerly chasing the full implications of advice—is a strong predictor of startup success.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that the most ambitious startup ideas are paradoxically repellent because they feel identity-threatening, yet this very reaction makes them invisible to most founders. He illustrates this with a set of huge, plausible startup directions, then concludes that the best way to pursue massive outcomes is to start small, approach obliquely, and expand once you have a strong foothold.
key_points: Truly big startup ideas feel scary and tend to be filtered out by founders’ subconscious.
Ambitious ideas often succeed by first winning a small, intense user base rather than attacking the whole market.
Email, universities, entertainment distribution, hardware innovation, parallel computing, and medical diagnosis are ripe for disruptive startups despite institutional resistance.
Replacing entrenched systems requires reframing and incremental entry rather than declaring a frontal assault.
Visionary success often comes from a blurry direction and adaptive iteration, not a fixed blueprint.
money_quotes: The biggest startup ideas are terrifying.
The best ideas are just on the right side of impossible.
Email is not a messaging protocol.  It's a todo list.
Empirically, the way to do really big things seems to be to start with deceptively small things.
The popular image of the visionary is someone with a clear view of the future, but empirically it may be better to have a blurry one.
posts.key_insight: The most transformative startups succeed by tackling terrifyingly big problems through small, pragmatic footholds rather than bold declarations.

posts.summary: Graham argues that what counts as property is not fixed; it changes based on what works given technological and social conditions. Digital distribution makes content behave like air or smells, so attempts to enforce old property rules through heavy-handed law warp society and backfire. Societies should adapt definitions of property to new realities rather than entrench obsolete models for short-term gains.
key_points: Property is a pragmatic definition that shifts with technology and social feasibility.
Digital content now moves freely, so forcing scarcity through law distorts society.
Legacy media industries pursue short-term revenue, resisting necessary adaptation.
Healthy democracies and multiple sovereigns help prevent capture of property law.
money_quotes: What counts as property depends on what works to treat as property.
Data moves like smells now.
People should be able to charge for content when it works to charge for content.

When you're abusing the legal system by trying to use mass lawsuits against randomly chosen people as a form of exemplary punishment, or lobbying for laws that would break the Internet if they passed, that's ipso facto evidence you're using a definition of property that doesn't work.
It would be a disaster if we all had to keep running an obsolete version just because a few powerful people were too lazy to upgrade.
posts.key_insight: Property rules should evolve with technology, and attempts to enforce outdated definitions through coercive law harm society more than they protect creators.

posts.summary: Graham argues that writing quality depends mostly on having good ideas, while speaking skill depends far less on substance and more on performance. Seeing entertaining, famous speakers who said little convinced him that he values ideas over oratory and does not want to optimize for speaking ability.
key_points: Writing quality is driven primarily by the strength of the ideas.
Speaking can be impressive even when the content is thin.
Watching skilled speakers revealed how little ideas can matter in talks.
He prefers having good ideas to being a polished speaker.
Great speaking can even push against substantive thinking.
money_quotes: What I really want is to have good ideas, and that's a much bigger part of being a good writer than being a good speaker.
Having good ideas is most of writing well.
With speaking it's the opposite: having good ideas is an alarmingly small component of being a good speaker.
it was only then I realized he hadn't said very much.
After about ten sentences I found myself thinking "I don't want to be a good speaker."
posts.key_insight: Good ideas are the core of strong writing, while exceptional speaking can succeed even when substance is thin.

posts.summary: Paul Graham recounts how Y Combinator began from a casual realization about fixing venture funding and a desire to work with trusted collaborators. The essay argues that YC’s defining innovations—standardized seed terms and especially synchronized batch funding—emerged quickly, worked better than expected, and were amplified by lucky timing, strong early founders, and the move to Silicon Valley.
key_points: YC started as a small, founder-led fund built around smaller, standardized seed investments.
The most important breakthrough was funding startups synchronously in batches.
Early success came from unexpectedly strong founders and low initial expectations.
YC’s credibility lagged behind performance, mirroring how “toy” ideas can be undervalued.
money_quotes: Between Harvard Square and my house the idea gelled.
So YC started with $200k.
That's why I knew there was room for something like Y Combinator.
We also got lucky in who the first batch of founders were.
That's one of the reasons we especially like funding ideas that might be dismissed as "toys" — because YC itself was dismissed as one initially.
posts.key_insight: YC’s success came from rethinking seed funding into standardized, synchronous batches and then learning fast from the first cohort.

posts.summary: Graham reflects on Bronnie Ware's list of end-of-life regrets and sees how easily he could fall into the same errors of omission. He argues that these regrets stem from default behaviors and proposes countering them by making explicit, daily reminders that invert the regrets into commands.
key_points: The most dangerous regrets come from what you fail to do by default.
Modern life pressures people into shrinking themselves to fit circumstances.
Changing defaults is hard, so deliberate reminders are necessary.
money_quotes: If you had to compress them into a single piece of advice, it might be: don't be a cog.
The 5 regrets paint a portrait of post-industrial man, who shrinks himself into a shape that fits his circumstances, then turns dutifully till he stops.
Errors of omission are a particularly dangerous type of mistake, because you make them by default.
As long as these mistakes happen by default, you probably have to be reminded not to make them.
Don't ignore your dreams; don't work too much; say what you think; cultivate friendships; be happy.
posts.key_insight: To avoid the quiet regrets that come from default living, make your deepest values explicit and keep them in front of you every day.

posts.summary: The essay argues that a startup is defined by being designed to grow fast; everything else associated with startups flows from that single goal. Rapid growth requires large markets and scalable delivery, which pushes founders toward novel ideas, relentless measurement of growth rates, and decisions optimized for compounding. Understanding growth also explains the roles of VCs, acquisitions, and the high failure rate of startups.
key_points: Startups are distinct from ordinary small businesses because they are built for rapid scale.
Growth rate is the core metric and can guide nearly every decision a founder makes.
Big, scalable ideas are scarce, so successful startups often come from founders seeing overlooked problems.
Exponential growth makes small differences in weekly rates lead to huge differences in outcomes.
VC funding and acquisitions are rational responses to the value and threat created by fast growth.
money_quotes: A startup is a company designed to grow fast.
The only essential thing is growth.
If there's one number every founder should always know, it's the company's growth rate.
Optimizing for growth is a kind of evolutionary pressure that can actually discover startup ideas.
If you want to understand startups, understand growth.
posts.key_insight: Startups are defined by rapid, measurable growth, and using growth as the compass shapes ideas, decisions, funding, and outcomes.

posts.summary: Graham argues that startup investing is dominated by extreme power-law outcomes, where a tiny number of companies generate nearly all returns. This reality is counterintuitive because the best ideas often look bad at first, and common signals like fundraising success are misleading. To do well, investors must ignore gut instincts and focus on the slim chance of outsized wins.
key_points: Returns in startup investing are concentrated in a few massive winners, often 1000x outcomes.
Ideas that become the biggest successes frequently look like bad ideas at first glance.
Fundraising success after Demo Day is a misleading metric and can correlate inversely with true outliers.
Investors should take more risk than conventional signals suggest, focusing on potential for huge impact.
Human intuition struggles with power-law distributions, making disciplined risk-taking essential.
money_quotes: The two most important things to understand about startup investing, as a business, are (1) that effectively all the returns are concentrated in a few big winners, and (2) that the best ideas look initially like bad ideas.
You need to do what you know intellectually to be right, even though it feels wrong.
History tends to get rewritten by big successes, so that in retrospect it seems obvious they were going to make it big.
Fundraising is not merely a useless metric, but positively misleading.

The best we can hope for is that when we interview a group and find ourselves thinking "they seem like good founders, but what are investors going to think of this crazy idea?" we'll continue to be able to say "who cares what investors think?"
posts.key_insight: In a power-law world like startups, the right strategy is to embrace counterintuitive risks and back ideas that look wrong but could be insanely right.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that hardware startups are resurging, evidenced by YC’s recent batch where hardware companies outperformed non-hardware peers despite investor bias. He attributes the shift to multiple enabling forces—crowdfunding, better manufacturing and prototyping tools, ubiquitous connectivity, and online distribution—making hardware more viable for fast growth. He concludes that founders should ignore investor prejudice and pursue hardware now because the old software advantage may be temporary.
key_points: YC observed a notable rise in hardware startups, which performed strongly in a recent batch.
Investor bias against hardware lags reality; founders often see the future sooner because they build it.
Multiple technology and market shifts make hardware easier to prototype, manufacture, and sell.
Hardware has always been compelling; its weakness for rapid growth may be fading.
Founders should not let investor discrimination deter them, especially when applying to YC.
money_quotes: Investors' opinions are a trailing indicator.
The best founders are better at seeing the future than the best investors, because the best founders are making it.
Physical things are great.
Maybe the advantage of software will turn out to have been temporary.
It wouldn't be the first time something was a bad idea till it wasn't.
posts.key_insight: Hardware startups are becoming viable for rapid growth again, so founders should pursue them despite lingering investor bias.

posts.summary: Graham argues that the best startup ideas aren’t fabricated on demand but noticed by founders who are already living at the leading edge of a changing field. Great ideas come from solving real, urgent problems you personally feel, start with a narrow but deep “well” of demand, and expand from there. The practical recipe is to become the kind of person who can notice what’s missing, then build what seems interesting and needed.
key_points: Work on problems you personally feel to ensure real demand and avoid “sitcom” ideas.
Prefer deep, urgent need in a small group over shallow interest in a large market.
Good ideas are noticed, not invented; live at the leading edge to see what’s missing.
Turn off the unsexy and schlep filters; messy problems often hide valuable ideas.
Early niches matter only if there’s a plausible path to a much larger market.
money_quotes: The way to get startup ideas is not to try to think of startup ideas.
The verb you want to be using with respect to startup ideas is not "think up" but "notice."
Live in the future, then build what's missing.
Live in the future and build what seems interesting.
A crowded market is actually a good sign, because it means both that there's demand and that none of the existing solutions are good enough.
posts.key_insight: The most reliable way to find a great startup idea is to live at the leading edge, notice what’s missing in your own life, and build for a small group that urgently needs it.

posts.summary: Graham argues that startups rarely take off on their own; founders must deliberately push them by doing unscalable, labor-intensive work early on. Manual user recruiting, intense customer delight, and narrow initial markets create the momentum and feedback loops that later allow scaling.
key_points: Early growth usually requires founders to recruit users manually and provide hands-on service.
Small numbers matter because compound weekly growth turns modest starts into large outcomes.
Delighting early users is both a growth lever and a critical feedback loop for product quality.
Deliberately narrow markets can create critical mass before expansion.
Big launches and partnerships are poor substitutes for sustained, unscalable effort.
money_quotes: Actually startups take off because the founders make them take off.
A lot of would-be founders believe that startups either take off or don't.
Your first users should feel that signing up with you was one of the best choices they ever made.
Over-engaging with early users is not just a permissible technique for getting growth rolling.
It's not enough just to do something extraordinary initially.  You have to make an extraordinary effort initially.
posts.key_insight: Early-stage startups win by doing unscalable, hands-on work to recruit and delight users, creating the momentum and feedback needed to scale later.

posts.summary: Graham argues that convincing investors isn’t about pitching harder but about understanding and truthfully explaining why your startup is a good bet. Investors hunt for power-law outcomes and decide quickly based on founders’ formidability, market size/capturability, and evidence so far. The most reliable path is to convince yourself first, then communicate that case clearly and concisely.
key_points: Investors treat big success as binary and use founder formidability as the primary filter.
Seeming formidable comes from telling truths you deeply understand, not swagger or salesmanship.
A good bet requires a plausible path to owning a big market, often via a small market and a “why now” trend.
Raise money when you can convincingly explain the bet, not when a deadline or need forces it.
money_quotes: Convince yourself that your startup is worth investing in, and then when you explain this to investors they'll believe you.
The time to raise money is not when you need it, or when you reach some artificial deadline like a Demo Day.
Founders think of startups as ideas, but investors think of them as markets.
Make the truth good, then just tell it.
posts.key_insight: The most convincing pitch is a clear, truthful explanation of why your startup is a good bet, grounded in deep understanding rather than performance.

posts.summary: Graham explains that investor interest is heavily driven by other investors’ interest, creating herd dynamics that can cause stampedes or prolonged indifference. He argues this is less a signal of startup quality than a byproduct of risk reduction, founder confidence, and investors’ difficulty judging startups, and advises founders to understand these forces so they aren’t surprised or demoralized.
key_points: Investor opinion is strongly influenced by other investors’ interest, creating feedback loops.
Raising money genuinely reduces risk and can justify higher valuations for later investors.
Investor behavior is shaped by founders’ confidence and by mediocre investors copying others.
Deadlines created by competing offers can prompt decisions, but founders should avoid manipulation.
Early fundraising is hardest because the herd stays distant; the second commitment is easier.
money_quotes: The biggest component in most investors' opinion of you is the opinion of other investors.

Lots of startups that cause stampedes end up flaming out (in extreme cases, partly as a result of the stampede), and lots of very successful startups were only moderately popular with investors the first time they raised money.
After you raise the first million dollars, the company is at least a million dollars more valuable, because it's the same company as before, plus it has a million dollars in the bank.
Judging startups is hard even for the best investors.  The mediocre ones might as well be flipping coins.
So don't be demoralized by how hard it is to get the first commitment, because much of the difficulty comes from this external force.
posts.key_insight: Investor herd dynamics distort fundraising signals, so founders should understand the feedback loops and not mistake a stampede—or early indifference—for the true quality of the startup.

posts.summary: Graham argues that fundraising is difficult but predictable if founders follow a simple, disciplined process that counteracts investor incentives and founder wishful thinking. The core advice is to fundraise in focused bursts, run a breadth‑first process weighted by expected value, treat anything short of a firm offer as a no, and close money quickly so you can return to building the company. He emphasizes that fundraising is a means to growth and profitability, not a measure of success, and warns against over-optimizing valuation or raising too much.
key_points: Fundraising should be an all‑consuming, time‑boxed mode; otherwise avoid investor meetings.
Investors are driven by fear of missing out and fear of backing a flop, which makes their signals unreliable unless backed by commitments.
Run parallel conversations weighted by expected value, and treat soft interest as a no until a definite offer appears.
Get the first meaningful commitment fast, then close money quickly to avoid reversals.
Optimize for speed, good investors, and sufficient capital; valuation is secondary and raising too much can be harmful.
money_quotes: Fundraising is hard in both senses: hard like lifting a heavy weight, and hard like solving a puzzle.
Treat investors as saying no till they unequivocally say yes, in the form of a definite offer with no contingencies.
The optimal solution is to talk to all potential investors in parallel, but give higher priority to the more promising ones.
Fundraising is not what will make you successful.  It's just a means to an end.
If you want to raise money, the best thing you can do is get yourself to the point where you don't need to.
posts.key_insight: Run fundraising as a focused, parallel process and treat only firm commitments as real, because the fastest way to success is to close acceptable money and get back to building.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that startups are counterintuitive and can’t be mastered by imitating startup mechanics or gaming systems; the real path is making something people want and trusting good judgment about people. For students, the best preparation is not “entrepreneurship” but deep learning and curiosity, because startups are all‑consuming and ideas emerge from genuine engagement with real problems.
key_points: Startups are counterintuitive; instincts often mislead, except about people where gut judgment matters.
Expertise in users and problems matters more than expertise in startup mechanics or fundraising tricks.
Gaming the system stops working in startups; only real value to users counts.
Starting a startup is all-consuming and usually not optimal while in college.
Good startup ideas arise from curiosity-driven work on interesting, real problems, often as side projects.
money_quotes: Startups are as unnatural as skiing, so there's a similar list for startups.
The way to succeed in a startup is not to be an expert on startups, but to be an expert on your users and the problem you're solving for them.
There is no boss to trick, only users, and all users care about is whether your product does what they want.
Starting a successful startup is similar to having kids in that it's like a button you push that changes your life irrevocably.
So here is the ultimate advice for young would-be startup founders, boiled down to two words: just learn.
posts.key_insight: The best way to prepare for a startup is to cultivate deep curiosity and real problem expertise, because in the end only making something people want matters.

posts.summary: Graham observes that among the most successful people he knows, meanness is surprisingly uncommon, even though mean behavior is widespread. The internet has made that meanness more visible by letting everyone publish their opinions, underscoring how common it is despite its apparent mismatch with long-term success.
key_points: Successful people are rarely mean, with only a few exceptions.
Meanness is common and highly visible online.
Broader access to publishing has revealed how widespread mean behavior is.
money_quotes: It struck me recently how few of the most successful people I know are mean.
There are exceptions, but remarkably few.
Meanness isn't rare.
In fact, one of the things the internet has shown us is how mean people can be.
A few decades ago, only famous people and professional writers got to publish their opinions.
posts.key_insight: Meanness may be common, but it is notably absent among the most successful people.

posts.summary: Graham argues that tech companies are right to push for easier immigration because exceptional programmers are rare and cannot be produced just through training. Since the US has only about 5% of the world’s population, most great programmers are born elsewhere, and letting them in is crucial to maintaining US technological leadership. He warns that failing to do so could shift the global talent hub away from the US with long-term consequences.
key_points: Exceptional programmers are orders of magnitude more valuable and cannot be produced by training alone.
Given population realities, most great programmers are born outside the US.
Tech companies seek immigration reform because of genuine talent scarcity, not wage suppression.
Concentrations of top talent create powerful hubs, so attracting them is strategically vital.
money_quotes: The technology companies are right.
Exceptional performance implies immigration.
We have the potential to ensure that the US remains a technology superpower just by letting in a few thousand great programmers a year.
It could easily be the defining mistake this generation of American politicians later become famous for.
So please, get on with it.
posts.key_insight: Because exceptional programmers are rare and globally distributed, immigration is the practical lever that determines whether the US remains a top technology hub.

posts.summary: Graham argues that expertise decays in a changing world because confidence built on past experience becomes obsolete. The best defense is to assume change, stay aggressively open-minded, treat beliefs as provisional, and listen to the signals that come from weird hunches and the people most likely to generate new ideas.
key_points: Expertise can become a liability when the world changes faster than beliefs update.
Predicting change is hard, so adopt open-mindedness and treat beliefs as flexible hypotheses.
New ideas come from solving problems and exploring odd hunches rather than forcing originality.
Betting on ideas (with real consequences) improves accuracy more than casual commentary.
Focus on people with traits that reliably produce new ideas, not just on the ideas themselves.
money_quotes: When experts are wrong, it's often because they're experts on an earlier version of the world.
The first step is to have an explicit belief in change.
The best strategy is simply to be aggressively open-minded.
Any weird idea or apparently irrelevant question that occurs to you is ipso facto worth exploring.
Surround yourself with the sort of people new ideas come from.
posts.key_insight: In fast-changing domains, the surest path to staying right is to treat beliefs as temporary, stay open to weird signals, and align with people who generate the future.

posts.summary: Graham argues that reading and experience reshape your mental models even when you forget the specific details, so the value of books isn’t measured by what you can recall. This explains why rereading matters: the same material is “compiled” differently at different times in life. He extends the idea to future tech, suggesting we may eventually replay and even edit experiences to relearn from them.
key_points: Forgetting details doesn’t erase the lasting effect reading has on your mental models.
Knowledge is like a compiled program whose source you no longer remember.
Rereading important books is valuable because your mind compiles them differently over time.
Future technologies could let us replay or edit experiences to extract new learning.
Not knowing how you know things may be contingent on current human limitations.
money_quotes: Reading and experience train your model of the world.
Your mind is like a compiled program you've lost the source of.
The same book would get compiled differently at different points in your life.
Whereas now the phrase "already read" seems almost ill-formed.
So although not knowing how you know things may seem part of being human, it may not be.
posts.key_insight: Even when you forget what you read, it still reshapes your mental models—making rereading and re-experiencing powerful ways to deepen understanding.

posts.summary: Graham argues that many startups hit a “fatal pinch” where they still have cash but burn is high, growth is weak, and the assumption of easy follow-on funding is a dangerous delusion. Because this phase is self‑reinforcing, founders must act as if no more money is coming and focus immediately on profitability through cutting costs, increasing revenue, or shutting down.
key_points: The fatal pinch is a late-stage cash-and-burn trap where weak growth makes fundraising unlikely.
Assuming investors will rescue you is the fatal mistake; the real probability is effectively zero.
The situation is self‑reinforcing: optimism about funding delays actions that could save the company.
Survival requires ruthless focus on profitability via cuts, revenue shifts, or closing down.
Consulting-like work can be a temporary lifeline if it brings fast, high‑value revenue while protecting IP.
money_quotes: That last sentence is the fatal one.
I will now, by an amazing feat of clairvoyance, do this for you: the probability is zero.
What good will more code do you when you're out of business?
Be ruthlessly mercenary when you start doing this, though.
You just have to realize in time that you're near death.
posts.key_insight: In a cash‑burning stall, assume fundraising is off the table and act fast to reach profitability or shut down.

posts.summary: Graham argues that startups should avoid talking to corporate development unless they are ready to sell right now and likely to get an acceptable offer. The biggest risk is for promising early-stage companies, where corp dev conversations create distractions, morale hits, and manipulative dynamics that can push founders toward a premature sale.
key_points: Corp dev exists to buy companies; if you are not ready to sell now, do not take the meeting.
The middle stage is most vulnerable: fast-growing but still small startups face the highest risk.
Corp dev processes are long, distracting, and typically end in low offers that sap morale.
Acquirers may use aggressive tactics to lower price and shift leverage onto founders.
The safest protection is never taking the first meeting unless you truly want to sell.
money_quotes: It's usually a mistake to talk to corp dev unless (a) you want to sell your company right now and (b) you're sufficiently likely to get an offer at an acceptable price.
Distractions are the thing you can least afford in a startup.
If you get an offer at all, it will be at the end of a long and unbelievably distracting process. And if the offer is surprising, it will be surprisingly low.
If they can, corp dev people like to turn the tables on you. They like to get you to the point where you're trying to convince them to buy instead of them trying to convince you to sell.
Do you want to sell your company right now? Not eventually, right now. If not, just don't take the first meeting.
posts.key_insight: If you are not ready to sell immediately, taking a corp dev meeting is a high-cost distraction that can weaken your resolve and leverage.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that Ron Conway’s success as an investor comes from genuinely being good, not just seeming good, because deals flow through trust and referrals. As the startup world has become more transparent and unpredictable, it’s increasingly hard to fake goodness, so the most successful investors tend to be the most upstanding.
key_points: Ron Conway’s benevolence directly drives access to top deals through referrals.
