Transparency makes bad behavior visible and costly for investors.
Unpredictability makes strategic niceness impractical, favoring genuine goodness.
The startup world has shifted toward rewarding real integrity more than in the past.
money_quotes: It was by being benevolent that Ronco became so powerful.
In a sufficiently connected and unpredictable world, you can't seem good without being good.
The random college kid you talk to today might in a couple years be the CEO of the hottest startup in the Valley.
Though plenty of investors are jerks, there is a clear trend among them: the most successful investors are also the most upstanding.
As often happens, Ron discovered how to be the investor of the future by accident.
posts.key_insight: In today’s startup ecosystem, real integrity is not just moral—it’s a competitive advantage that compounds through trust and referrals.

posts.summary: Graham reflects on his mathematician father to argue that what feels like work to others but not to you is a strong signal of what you are well suited to do. He suggests that unusual enjoyment of tasks (like debugging or writing papers for others) provides subtle clues about the kind of work you should pursue. The essay encourages explicitly asking this question to uncover a fitting path.
key_points: Enjoying tasks others find tedious is a reliable indicator of personal fit.
Unusual tastes can be stronger evidence of what you should do than conventional preferences.
Many people must infer their best work from subtle signals rather than early certainty.
money_quotes: If something that seems like work to other people doesn't seem like work to you, that's something you're well suited for.
The stranger your tastes seem to other people, the stronger evidence they probably are of what you should do.
What seems like work to other people that doesn't seem like work to you?
To him the problems were the reward.
So I bet it would help a lot of people to ask themselves about this explicitly.
posts.key_insight: Your best-fit work is often revealed by tasks that feel unusually enjoyable or effortless to you while seeming like drudgery to others.

posts.summary: Graham argues that many of the biggest startups looked laughably small or even wrong at launch, and the key is to explain why those “lame” ideas weren’t actually lame. Founders often can’t articulate the full potential because they’re driven by instinct and proximity to the future, so evaluators should ask what big company the idea could become and treat it like a puzzle.
key_points: Most successful startups seemed absurdly lame at the start, not merely small.
Founders are often guided by instinct and future proximity rather than a clear narrative.
Reframing a lame idea as “the Altair Basic of” a future giant reveals hidden potential.
There may be multiple plausible growth paths; follow the most exciting one.
money_quotes: One of the most valuable exercises you can try if you want to understand startups is to look at the most successful companies and explain why they were not as lame as they seemed when they first launched.
These ideas didn't just seem small.  They seemed wrong.  They were the kind of ideas you could not merely ignore, but ridicule.
Often the founders themselves didn't know why their ideas were promising.
When I encounter a startup with a lame-sounding idea, I ask "What Microsoft is this the Altair Basic of?"
Their instincts got them this far. Why stop now?
posts.key_insight: Seemingly lame startup ideas often hide the seeds of major companies, so the right approach is to ask what future giant they could become and trust founder instincts when the path is still unclear.

posts.summary: Graham argues that if a US startup can't secure the .com for its name, it should probably rename itself. The primary issue isn't discoverability but the signal of weakness: a second-tier domain implies a second-tier company unless the brand is already huge.
key_points: Lacking the .com for a startup's name is a strong negative signal.
Domain names matter less for user acquisition but more for perceived legitimacy.
Only very well-known companies can overcome the stigma of a marginal domain.
money_quotes: If you have a US startup called X and you don't have x.com, you should probably change your name.
The problem with not having the .com of your name is that it signals weakness.
Unless you're so big that your reputation precedes you, a marginal domain suggests you're a marginal company.
posts.key_insight: For most startups, lacking the .com version of their name undermines perceived credibility enough to justify changing the name.

posts.summary: Paul Graham says a founder email clarified why startup founders can be nice without being disadvantaged. He contrasts a childhood cartoon of ruthless businessmen with what he has learned, implying that success in startups doesn’t require being rapacious.
key_points: A founder’s email sparked a realization about niceness being safe in startups.
Graham contrasts a stereotypical ruthless businessman with real-world startup dynamics.
money_quotes: I recently got an email from a founder that helped me understand something important: why it's safe for startup founders to be nice people.

I grew up with a cartoon idea of a very successful businessman (in the cartoon it was always a man): a rapacious, cigar-smoking, table-thumping guy in his fifties who wins by exercising power, and isn't too fussy about how.
posts.key_insight: Startup success doesn’t require ruthlessness; founders can be genuinely nice and still win.

posts.summary: Graham argues that startups should evaluate whether they are “default alive” or “default dead” based on current cash, expenses, and growth, because the answer determines whether to pursue ambition or survival. Founders often ignore this question too long, mistakenly betting on fundraising, and fall into the fatal pinch of slow growth and insufficient runway. The best way to avoid it is to delay hiring, focus on product appeal, and have a concrete plan B.
key_points: Default alive vs. default dead should be asked early and revisited as companies mature.
Relying on fundraising is risky; treat it as plan A with a written plan B.
Fast growth usually comes from product-market fit, not high spending.
Overhiring is the biggest killer of funded startups and often masks weak product appeal.
Founders should prioritize survival and product evolution over scaling prematurely.
money_quotes: Assuming their expenses remain constant and their revenue growth is what it has been over the last several months, do they make it to profitability on the money they have left?
Half the founders I talk to don't know whether they're default alive or default dead.
The fatal pinch is default dead + slow growth + not enough time to fix it.
Hiring too fast is by far the biggest killer of startups that raise money.
At this early stage, the product needs to evolve more than to be "built out," and that's usually easier with fewer people.
posts.key_insight: Startups survive by knowing early whether their current trajectory leads to profitability and by resisting the urge to hire and fundraise instead of fixing product appeal.

posts.summary: Graham argues you can detect bias in a selection process without knowing the applicant pool by examining whether selected members of a group outperform others. If a group faces a higher bar, those who pass it should have higher measured performance, assuming the performance metric is valid and not itself biased. He uses VC funding and First Round Capital’s data on female founders as an example of how this reveals bias in selection.
key_points: Bias implies a higher selection threshold for a group, so selected members should outperform peers
The method works when you have a random sample of selected applicants and valid performance measures
Publicly available selection data enables third parties to detect bias even without applicant pool data
VC portfolio performance can reveal selection bias against female founders
money_quotes: What it means for a selection process to be biased against applicants of type x is that it's harder for them to make it through.
Which means applicants of type x who do make it through the selection process will outperform other successful applicants.
Want to know if the selection process was biased against some type of applicant? Check whether they outperform the others.
This is not just a heuristic for detecting bias. It's what bias means.
I predict we'll see this technique used more in the future.
posts.key_insight: If a group faces a higher bar in selection, their selected members should outperform others, and that performance gap is direct evidence of bias.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that the easiest way to make writing clearer and more engaging is to use spoken language. Written prose often becomes overly formal and complex, which both tires readers and misleads writers into thinking they are saying more. He recommends revising sentences to sound like conversation, even reading drafts aloud, to keep ideas direct and accessible.
key_points: Written language often becomes more complex and formal than speech, which makes it harder to read and easier to misunderstand.
Complex ideas do not require complex sentences; experts tend to speak informally when discussing hard topics.
A practical method is to revise each sentence to match how you would say it to a friend, or even replace drafts with spoken explanations.
money_quotes: Here's a simple trick for getting more people to read what you write: write in spoken language.
You don't need complex sentences to express complex ideas.
Informal language is the athletic clothing of ideas.
Before I publish a new essay, I read it out loud and fix everything that doesn't sound like conversation.
If you simply manage to write in spoken language, you'll be ahead of 95% of writers.
posts.key_insight: Clarity and engagement in writing come from sounding like conversation, so revise until every sentence is something you’d actually say aloud.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that YC was never a one-man show and that Jessica Livingston’s character judgment and culture-building were central to YC’s early success. He claims YC’s defining advantage was selecting founders well, and that Jessica’s “social radar” shaped the alumni network and the organization’s values. Her reluctance to seek attention, combined with public narratives, has obscured her role despite her outsized impact.
key_points: YC’s distinctive culture and founder selection were primarily shaped by Jessica Livingston.
Authenticity and character assessment were critical to YC’s success, especially at the earliest stages.
Avoiding bad actors strengthened the YC network and long-term value.
Jessica’s aversion to attention and public conflict led to her contributions being underrecognized.
Her insights about founders are among the most consequential for startup outcomes and economic growth.
money_quotes: If you don't understand her, you don't understand YC.
At YC, the culture was the product.
Authenticity is one of the most important things YC looks for in founders.
We thought Airbnb was a bad idea. We funded it because we liked the founders.
The person who knows the most about the most important factor in the growth of mature economies — that is who Jessica Livingston is.
posts.key_insight: YC’s success hinged less on prescience about technology and more on Jessica Livingston’s ability to read founders and build a culture of authenticity.

posts.summary: Graham argues that economic inequality is not a single phenomenon and shouldn’t be treated as one; it has multiple causes, some harmful and some beneficial, like wealth created by technology and startups. He contends that eliminating inequality would require blocking wealth creation itself, which would be both impractical and harmful, and urges focusing on specific problems like poverty and corruption instead.
key_points: Economic inequality mixes distinct causes; collapsing them leads to bad policy and analysis.
Wealth creation via technology and startups is a major, persistent source of inequality and is not zero-sum.
Cracking down on rent-seeking is good but won’t remove large wealth gaps because productivity gains keep expanding.
Trying to eliminate large wealth variation would mean eliminating startups and driving ambition elsewhere.
Policy should target concrete harms like poverty, lack of mobility, and corruption rather than the inequality metric itself.
money_quotes: The most common mistake people make about economic inequality is to treat it as a single phenomenon.
You can't prevent great variations in wealth without preventing people from getting rich, and you can't do that without preventing them from starting startups.
The evolution of technology is one of the most powerful forces in history.
Economic inequality is sufficiently far from identical with the various problems that have it as a symptom that we'll probably only hit whichever of the two we aim at.
If we want to fix the world behind the statistics, we have to understand it, and focus our efforts where they'll do the most good.
posts.key_insight: Inequality is a mixed-output metric driven in part by productive wealth creation, so policy should target specific harms like poverty and rent-seeking rather than trying to suppress the entire distribution.

posts.summary: Graham argues that many modern trends toward polarization, cultural divergence, geographic clustering, and inequality are all instances of a single phenomenon: society is re-fragmenting as the unusually strong forces that once pushed Americans together have eroded. Those forces were World War II and the rise of large, national corporations, and he contends they were a historical anomaly we wouldn’t want to recreate.
money_quotes: I'd like to propose a hypothesis: that all these trends are instances of the same phenomenon.
And moreover, that the cause is not some force that's pulling us apart, but rather the erosion of forces that had been pushing us together.

Worse still, for those who worry about these trends, the forces that were pushing us together were an anomaly, a one-time combination of circumstances that's unlikely to be repeated — and indeed, that we would not want to repeat.
The two forces were war (above all World War II), and the rise of large corporations.
What happens now with the Super Bowl used to happen every night. We were literally in sync.
posts.key_insight: Today’s fragmentation is less a new divisive force than the fading of the extraordinary unifying pressures of wartime mobilization and mid‑century corporate consolidation.

posts.summary: Graham argues that life is genuinely short, a fact made vivid by seeing time in discrete chunks through parenthood. Because of that, we should aggressively eliminate “bullshit” that wastes time, prioritize what truly matters, and act before opportunities and people are gone.
key_points: Life’s shortness becomes clear when time is seen in limited, countable experiences.
“Life is too short for x” should be treated as a real decision rule, not a figure of speech.
Bullshit enters life by force or by trickery, and both should be actively minimized.
What matters varies by person and is often learned slowly through experience.
Don’t wait to do what matters; savor time and experiences while you have them.
money_quotes: Relentlessly prune bullshit, don't wait to do things that matter, and savor the time you have.
If you find yourself thinking that life is too short for something, you should try to eliminate it if you can.
It's the junk food of experience.
Counterintuitive as it feels, it's better most of the time not to defend yourself.
Just don't wait.
posts.key_insight: Treat time as scarce: cut the fake and draining, choose what matters, and act now while the window is open.

posts.summary: Graham argues Pittsburgh can become a startup hub by doubling down on its unique strengths: attracting young talent, preserving livable neighborhoods, and leveraging CMU as a top-tier research magnet. He stresses that cultural tolerance and city personality matter as much as capital, and that investor ecosystems grow slowly but are less of a bottleneck today. The path is gradual, but realistic if Pittsburgh becomes even more itself.
key_points: Attracting 25–29 year olds is a leading indicator for startup density and talent wars.
City personality (independent cafes, historic buildings, walkability) creates the environment founders want.
CMU’s best contribution is excellence in research, not entrepreneurship programs.
Tolerance and pragmatic culture are prerequisites for startup ecosystems.
Investor communities take time, but modern startups can bootstrap via lower costs, crowdfunding, and YC.
money_quotes: Startups are made of people, and the average age of the people in a typical startup is right in that 25 to 29 bracket.
These independent restaurants and cafes are not just feeding people. They're making there be a there here.
The way to get innovation is not to aim for innovation but to aim for something more specific, like better batteries or better 3D printing.
The empirical evidence, weighted by market cap, suggests the best thing they can do is literally nothing.
If Pittsburgh's path to becoming a startup hub is to be even more itself, then it has a good chance of succeeding.
posts.key_insight: Pittsburgh’s best route to becoming a startup hub is to amplify its existing strengths—livability, talent magnetism, and tolerant culture—while letting capital and startups grow organically over time.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that the death penalty debate in California isn’t about whether it’s acceptable to execute guilty killers, but about the reality that innocent people are sometimes convicted. Given systemic flaws in policing, defense, and prosecution, he frames the moral question as whether society should risk executing innocents and urges a yes vote on Proposition 62 and no on Proposition 66.
key_points: The practical debate is about the risk of executing innocent people, not abstract moral philosophy.
Systemic errors in policing, defense, and prosecution make wrongful convictions inevitable.
A nontrivial share of death row inmates are innocent, making the policy morally unacceptable.
California voters can end the death penalty by supporting Proposition 62 and rejecting Proposition 66.
money_quotes: The real world does not work like the version I was shown on TV growing up.
In the real world, about 4% of people sentenced to death are innocent.
So this is not about whether it's ok to kill killers. This is about whether it's ok to kill innocent people.
A child could answer that one for you.
So yes on 62, no on 66.
posts.key_insight: Because real-world justice systems convict innocents, the death penalty necessarily risks killing the innocent and should be ended.

posts.summary: The essay argues that we underestimate how much risk famous scientists took because biographies erase their mistakes and because successful choices now look obvious. Using Newton, Graham notes that in his time physics, alchemy, and theology appeared similarly promising, so his bets were all risky, even though only one paid off.
key_points: Biographies sanitize failures, making historical risk-taking seem smaller than it was.
Success makes past choices look inevitable even when they were uncertain.
Newton pursued multiple lines that looked equally promising in his era.
Risk is inherent in discovery because outcomes are unknowable in advance.
money_quotes: Because biographies of famous scientists tend to edit out their mistakes, we underestimate the degree of risk they were willing to take.
Because anything a famous scientist did that wasn't a mistake has probably now become the conventional wisdom, those choices don't seem risky either.
Maybe the smartness and the craziness were not as separate as we think.
In Newton's day the three problems seemed roughly equally promising.
Newton made three bets. One of them worked. But they were all risky.
posts.key_insight: Discovery looks inevitable only in hindsight; at the time, even great minds are making risky bets among uncertain options.

posts.summary: Graham argues that people who hold power without charisma often attract disproportionate criticism because they lack the social tools to defuse it. This is common among builder-type leaders, who may be the best fit for the role despite being less likable. The takeaway is to recognize this dynamic as human nature and not misread criticism as evidence of poor fit.
key_points: Power without charisma invites criticism that cannot be easily deflected.
Builder-type leaders can be the right choice even if they lack schmoozing skills.
There is no fix for this dynamic; awareness is the best response.
Being a magnet for criticism can signal that someone is right for the job.
money_quotes: People who are powerful but uncharismatic will tend to be disliked.
Their power makes them a target for criticism that they don't have the charisma to disarm.
And yet the builder-type CEO is (like Hillary) probably the best person for the job.
I don't think there is any solution to this problem. It's human nature.
Being a magnet for criticism is sometimes a sign not that someone is the wrong person for a job, but that they're the right one.
posts.key_insight: Criticism of uncharismatic leaders often reflects the social costs of power rather than a mismatch for the role.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that the most valuable insights are both general and surprising, but that combination is rare because such territory gets mined quickly. He suggests aiming for moderate insights by adding a small delta of novelty to very general ideas, accepting repetition and near-rediscovery as part of the process because even slight novelty has outsized value.
key_points: The highest-value ideas are both broadly applicable and unexpected, but that space is scarce.
Moderately valuable insights often come from adding a small amount of generality or novelty to what already exists.
Working with very general ideas means small novelty is enough to matter, even if it feels derivative.
Repeating yourself is inevitable and can increase the chance of finding a tiny but critical new angle.
“Not much new” can still be a significant achievement when the domain is highly general.
money_quotes: The most valuable insights are both general and surprising.
Ordinarily, the best that people can do is one without the other: either surprising without being general (e.g. gossip), or general without being surprising (e.g. platitudes).
Because these start out so general, you only need a small delta of novelty to produce a useful insight.
A small delta of novelty is all you'll be able to get most of the time.
"Not much new" is a real achievement when you're talking about the most general ideas.
posts.key_insight: Even tiny novelty in very general ideas can yield valuable insights, so persist despite repetition or claims of “not much new.”

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that great work requires a third ingredient beyond ability and determination: a disinterested, obsessive interest in a topic. This obsession both substitutes for grit and guides people down unpromising paths where new ideas hide, but only yields genius when the obsession is about something that matters, even if that is hard to predict in advance.
key_points: Obsessive interest is a proxy for ability and a substitute for determination because curiosity pulls you forward.
Great discoveries often come from following interests that look pointless or unpromising to outsiders.
What matters is uncertain and time-dependent, so pursuing private obsessions is risky but can yield outsized rewards.
Hedging with safer paths reduces downside but also reduces the chance of exceptional outcomes.
Unevenly distributed interests, not just ability, explain why excellence clusters in certain fields.
money_quotes: If I had to put the recipe for genius into one sentence, that might be it: to have a disinterested obsession with something that matters.
An obsessive interest in a topic is both a proxy for ability and a substitute for determination.
The paths that lead to new ideas tend to look unpromising.
They didn't discover the hidden paths that they did because they seemed promising, but because they couldn't help it.
It may be that to do great work, you also have to waste a lot of time.
posts.key_insight: Genius arises when deep, disinterested obsession locks onto something consequential, even though which interests will matter is uncertain.

posts.summary: Graham argues that genuinely new ideas often emerge from challenging widely held but mistaken assumptions, which makes their discoverers vulnerable to accusations of heresy. A culture that punishes heresy suppresses not just dissent but a wide web of adjacent ideas, creating large dead zones of unexplored thought. He concludes that these dead zones can be mined for novel insights by intentionally seeking out heresies.
key_points: Novel ideas tend to be non-obvious because they hide behind cherished mistaken assumptions.
Pouncing on heresy suppresses a broad network of related ideas, not just direct opposition.
The bigger and more absurd the assumption, the larger the dead zone of unexplored ideas it creates.
Looking for heresies is a productive strategy for finding new ideas.
money_quotes: If you discover something new, there's a significant chance you'll be accused of some form of heresy.
To discover new things, you have to work on ideas that are good but non-obvious; if an idea is obviously good, other people are probably already working on it.
When you suppress heresy, you silence not just your opponents but also everyone who contradicts you unintentionally.
Every cherished mistaken assumption has a dead zone of unexplored ideas around it.
If you're looking for new ideas, one way to find them is by looking for heresies.
posts.key_insight: Protecting cherished assumptions breeds intellectual dead zones, so seeking out heresies is a practical path to discovering new ideas.

posts.summary: Graham argues that in many fields, most work clusters around fashionable problems, leaving vast areas unexplored. Even imaginative people gravitate toward what’s trendy, so real opportunity lies in unfashionable or “played out” areas where a new approach can unlock disproportionate value. Loving the work itself helps you persist in these marginal spaces long enough to find something novel.
key_points: Fields are often far less explored than they appear because people work on similar fashionable problems.
Targeting unfashionable or supposedly exhausted areas can yield high value if you find a fresh approach.
Genuine love of the work protects against herd behavior and sustains long-term exploration.
money_quotes: Even the smartest, most imaginative people are surprisingly conservative when deciding what to work on.
People who would never dream of being fashionable in any other way get sucked into working on fashionable problems.
If you can find a new approach into a big but apparently played out field, the value of whatever you discover will be multiplied by its enormous surface area.
The best protection against getting drawn into working on the same things as everyone else may be to genuinely love what you're doing.
posts.key_insight: The biggest intellectual and practical gains often come from unfashionable problems, especially if you’re motivated by genuine curiosity rather than trends.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that his fear of having kids was based on a biased, negative sample of parenthood and a misunderstanding of what daily life with children is actually like. After becoming a parent, he found that the experience brings frequent moments of peace, friendship, and happiness that outweigh the real costs in time, freedom, and ambition.
key_points: His pre‑kids view was skewed by noticing parents only in stressful public moments.
Parenthood brings frequent, ordinary moments of deep contentment that are hard to describe.
Kids reduce productivity and constrain schedules, but meaningful work can still happen.
Having kids can lower personal ambition because attention shifts toward them.
Much of the freedom he valued before kids was unused and came with loneliness.
money_quotes: Something I dreaded turned out to be wonderful.

People don't talk about these much — the magic is hard to put into words, and all other parents know about them anyway — but one of the great things about having kids is that there are so many times when you feel there is nowhere else you'd rather be, and nothing else you'd rather be doing.
You don't just love them. They become your friends too.
But you can't let work spill promiscuously through your whole life, like I used to before I had kids.
I had plenty of happy times before I had kids. But if I count up happy moments, not just potential happiness but actual happy moments, there are more after kids than before.
posts.key_insight: Parenthood trades some freedom and productivity for a steady stream of everyday joy and meaning that he hadn’t understood from the outside.

posts.summary: Graham argues that the most damaging lesson from school is learning to optimize for grades by hacking tests rather than actually learning. Because authority-imposed tests are usually hackable, students are trained to chase proxies and carry that mindset into startups and work, where the real way to win is often simply doing good work. He suggests that recognizing and unlearning this habit frees individuals and society, especially as work increasingly rewards genuine outcomes over authority-approved signals.
key_points: Grades and tests often reward test-hacking over real learning, so students optimize for proxies.
Authority-imposed tests are usually hackable unless explicitly designed to be unhackable.
This mindset carries into startups, where founders overcomplicate and chase signals instead of building great products.
In many modern fields, especially startups and writing, you win by doing good work, not by hacking tests.
Unlearning the habit of test-hacking can redirect ambition toward work with real, unhackable outcomes.
money_quotes: The most damaging thing you learned in school wasn't something you learned in any specific class. It was learning to get good grades.
Getting a good grade in a class on x is so different from learning a lot about x that you have to choose one or the other, and you can't blame students if they choose grades.
The worst thing it does is to train you that the way to win is by hacking bad tests.
In fact, one of the most striking things about startups is the degree to which you win by simply doing good work.
Imagine what the world could look like if that happened.
posts.key_insight: Schools train people to hack proxy tests, but real success—especially in startups—comes from doing genuinely good work, so the key is to unlearn the test-hacking mindset.

posts.summary: Graham argues there are two kinds of political moderates: intentional trimmers who aim for the midpoint, and accidental moderates who arrive there by thinking independently on each issue. Intentional moderates mirror ideologues by acquiring positions in bulk, while accidental moderates choose their own questions and answers and often face more criticism from both sides.
key_points: Intentional moderates cluster near the middle on every issue; accidental moderates have a wide spread of views that average out.
Both ideologues and intentional moderates adopt positions in bulk rather than independently.
Accidental moderates may not care about many issues that dominate partisan debates, making their politics hard to map.
Open accidental moderates endure attacks from both left and right and lack the support of an orthodox group.
Independent-mindedness about ideas is essential for strong intellectual work; if politics intersects your work, independence or mediocrity follows.
money_quotes: There are two distinct ways to be politically moderate: on purpose and by accident.
The defining quality of an ideologue, whether on the left or the right, is to acquire one's opinions in bulk.
It is not merely a manipulative rhetorical trick to say "if you're not with us, you're against us," but often simply false.
Openly being an accidental moderate requires the most courage of all, because you get attacked from both right and left.
But if the ideas you use in your work intersect with the politics of your time, you have two choices: be an accidental moderate, or be mediocre.
posts.key_insight: Independent-minded thinkers become accidental moderates not by trimming but by reasoning issue by issue, and that independence is essential for serious work with ideas.

posts.summary: Graham argues that haters are essentially fanboys with the sign flipped: both are obsessive, identity-bound, and irrational about a famous person. Because haters are an automatic byproduct of fame and not a true dispute, the right response is to stop analyzing them and avoid engagement.
key_points: Haters and fanboys share the same obsessive, identity-driven pattern; only the valence differs.
Fame itself fuels haters, who often interpret success as fraud to reconcile talent with popularity.
Haters tend to be frustrated, unsuccessful people and rarely do great work.
The correct strategy is to treat haters like obsessive fans and not spend time trying to fix them.
Disputes are a false analogy for hater behavior; it is not about you.
money_quotes: Haters are just fanboys with the sign switched.
The mistake here is to think of the hater as someone you have a dispute with.
Knowing that haters are just fanboys with the sign bit flipped makes it much easier to deal with them.
If you have obsessive fans, do you spend any time wondering what makes them love you so much? No, you just think "some people are kind of crazy," and that's the end of it.
It's not you, it's them.
posts.key_insight: Treat haters as the negative mirror of fanboys and stop trying to rationalize or fix their behavior.

posts.summary: Graham argues that feeling like a noob is not a sign of ignorance but a marker of growth, because new environments and ideas expand what you know even while they make you feel incompetent. Our aversion to the noob feeling evolved for stable contexts, but in a rapidly changing world it can mislead us away from learning.
key_points: Feeling like a noob often correlates with entering new domains that increase overall knowledge.
Discomfort with being a noob evolved to push competence in familiar tasks, not to avoid novelty.
In modern life, avoiding the noob feeling can keep you from learning and progress.
money_quotes: I constantly feel like a noob.
the more of a noob you are locally, the less of a noob you are globally.
So the feeling of being a noob is inversely correlated with actual ignorance.
Our dislike of feeling like a noob is our brain telling us "Come on, come on, figure this out."
Though it feels unpleasant, and people will sometimes ridicule you for it, the more you feel like a noob, the better.
posts.key_insight: The discomfort of feeling like a noob is often evidence you are learning more, so in modern life you should seek it rather than avoid it.

posts.summary: Graham argues that essays should aim to be useful rather than merely persuasive, which means stating claims that are true, important, novel to readers, and as strong as possible without becoming false. He explains techniques for achieving this—rigorous self-editing, writing from topics you’ve thought deeply about, and skillful qualification—while noting that usefulness often provokes disagreement or misrepresentation. He also contends that the internet has opened vast unexplored territory for essayists, with most valuable ideas still undiscovered.
key_points: Useful writing maximizes four components: correctness, importance, novelty, and strength.
Rigorous self-editing and withholding weak ideas ensures published sentences are worth hearing.
Write about what matters to you and what surprises you to proxy importance and novelty for readers.
Skillful qualification lets you state ideas boldly without drifting into falsehood.
The internet makes essay writing widely accessible, leaving most essays and ideas still unwritten and undiscovered.
money_quotes: Useful writing makes claims that are as strong as they can be made without becoming false.
Useful writing tells people something true and important that they didn't already know, and tells them as unequivocally as possible.
You can't ensure that every idea you have is good, but you can ensure that every one you publish is, by simply not publishing the ones that aren't.
Brevity is the diction of command.
Nearly all of them are left to write.
posts.key_insight: An essay is most valuable when it delivers a true, important, and novel idea stated as strongly as possible without overreaching.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that confident but uninformed predictions are usually safe for public figures because outcomes are vague or far in the future, letting them escape accountability. The coronavirus was different: it quickly and clearly falsified bad predictions, exposing who speaks credibly and who doesn’t. He urges readers to remember this rare, sharp test of credibility.
key_points: Public figures often make false predictions without consequence because outcomes are fuzzy or delayed.
Epidemics rapidly and unequivocally falsify predictions, removing room for spin.
The pandemic exposed a pattern of confident commentary about things people don’t understand.
Such events are a powerful, uncommon test of credibility worth remembering.
money_quotes: An epidemic is different. It falsifies your predictions rapidly and unequivocally.
Instead they just continued to use their ordinary m.o., which, as the epidemic has made clear, is to talk confidently about things they don't understand.
An event like this is thus a uniquely powerful way of taking people's measure.
And the tide has just gone out like never before.
Now that we've seen the results, let's remember what we saw, because this is the most accurate test of credibility we're ever likely to have.
posts.key_insight: The pandemic provided a rare, decisive test that exposed who is truly credible because bad predictions were quickly and clearly disproven.

posts.summary: Graham argues that people can be mapped into four quadrants based on conventional vs. independent-mindedness and passive vs. aggressive temperament, and that these types persist across societies. He contends that aggressively conventional-minded people pose outsized risks by suppressing free inquiry, while aggressively independent-minded people drive new ideas, scientific progress, and successful startups. The health of institutions like universities depends on protecting space for independent thought, especially amid social-media-fueled conformity pressures.
key_points: Conformism has two key dimensions: conventional vs. independent-minded and passive vs. aggressive, yielding four stable personality types.
Aggressively conventional-minded individuals are prone to enforcing orthodoxy and disproportionately contribute to social harm.
Independent-minded people generate new ideas and require protection through norms of free inquiry and debate.
Censoring “bad” ideas is dangerous because enforcement is error-prone and restrictions spill over into adjacent fields.
Universities and similar institutions may be weakening as havens for independent thinkers, but new protective institutions can emerge.
money_quotes: To be a successful scientist, for example, it's not enough just to be right. You have to be right when everyone else is wrong.
So it's no coincidence that societies prosper only to the extent that they have customs for keeping the conventional-minded at bay.
The conventional-minded say, as they always do, that they don't want to shut down the discussion of all ideas, just the bad ones.
Having ideas in a world where some ideas are banned is like playing soccer on a pitch that has a minefield in one corner.
But I'm hopeful long term. The independent-minded are good at protecting themselves.
posts.key_insight: Societal progress depends on protecting aggressively independent-minded people and the free inquiry they need, because attempts to police “bad” ideas inevitably shrink the space for innovation and truth-seeking.

posts.summary: Graham argues that a common but overlooked form of privilege is “orthodox privilege,” where conventional-minded people assume it is safe for everyone to express opinions because they themselves only hold socially acceptable ones. This blinds them to the existence of true but currently unsayable ideas and fuels disputes like cancel culture, where they interpret punished speech as deserved. He suggests politeness—taking others at their word about what can’t be said—as a practical way to bridge the gap.
key_points: Conventional-minded people mistakenly generalize their safety in speaking to everyone, creating “orthodox privilege.”
History shows there are always true statements that are dangerous to say; our era is unlikely to be unique.
Orthodox privilege is hard to overcome because it requires becoming more independent-minded, not just learning facts.
Disagreements about cancel culture stem from whether people can imagine true but unsayable ideas.
A polite stance—accepting others’ claims about unsayable truths—can reduce conflict even without shared examples.
money_quotes: The more conventional-minded someone is, the more it seems to them that it's safe for everyone to express their opinions.
They literally can't imagine a true statement that would get you in trouble.
Surely it should at least be the default assumption that our time is not unique, and that there are true things you can't say now, just as there have always been.
People can overcome the blindness induced by most forms of privilege by learning more about whatever they're not. But they can't overcome orthodox privilege just by learning more.
If you believe there's nothing true that you can't say, then anyone who gets in trouble for something they say must deserve it.
posts.key_insight: Assuming speech is safe because it’s safe for you is a form of privilege that obscures the existence of true but unsayable ideas and distorts how we judge public punishment for speech.

posts.summary: Graham models how recurring wealth taxes erode a startup founder’s equity over a lifetime. Even low annual rates compound dramatically because the same assets are taxed repeatedly, so seemingly small percentages can consume a large share of ownership over decades.
key_points: A flat 1% wealth tax over 60 years leaves about 54.7% of stock, meaning 45% is taken.
At higher rates, lifetime erosion is extreme (e.g., 2% ~70%, 5% ~95% over 60 years).
A high exemption threshold reduces but does not eliminate the compounding effect on successful founders.
Wealth taxes differ from income taxes because they repeatedly tax the same asset, causing compounding.
The model uses optimistic assumptions for founders, so real effects could be larger.
money_quotes: A wealth tax of 1% means you get to keep 99% of your stock each year.
So a straight 1% wealth tax means the government will over the course of your life take 45% of your stock.
It may at first seem surprising that such apparently small tax rates produce such dramatic effects.
The reason wealth taxes have such dramatic effects is that they're applied over and over to the same money.
A wealth tax compounds.
posts.key_insight: Because wealth taxes are applied repeatedly to the same assets, even low rates compound into large lifetime losses for founders.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that the fear of making something lame blocks ambitious work because early versions often look unimpressive even to their creators. He says we need to treat new ideas as special cases, cultivate optimism, and adopt techniques that help us push through the crude early stage. Over time, better social customs and personal habits can retrain us to judge early work more accurately and keep going.
key_points: Early versions of ambitious projects often look weak, and fear of that stage prevents many from starting or continuing.
Silicon Valley’s custom of optimism toward strange ideas shows that social attitudes can shift to support new work.
People dismiss new ideas to seem clever or out of envy; this harms innovation more than honest skepticism.
Personal strategies like overconfidence, supportive peers, and reframing work as experiments help push through the lame phase.
Studying the early histories of great work can retrain our judgment and reduce fear of crude beginnings.
money_quotes: One of the biggest things holding people back from doing great work is the fear of making something lame.
The right way to deal with new ideas is to treat them as a challenge to your imagination — not just to have lower standards, but to switch polarity entirely.
Optimism becomes urgent.
A lame-looking early version of an ambitious project truly is more valuable than it seems.
Curiously enough, the solution to the problem of judging early work too harshly is to realize that our attitudes toward it are themselves early work.
posts.key_insight: Great work requires pushing past the embarrassingly crude early stage by reshaping both social expectations and personal judgment to see early efforts as necessary, valuable steps.

posts.summary: Graham argues that some kinds of work require independent-mindedness because success depends on having correct ideas that are also novel, unlike most work where being right is enough. He claims independent-mindedness is partly innate but can be cultivated by choosing the right environments, practicing skepticism, and nurturing curiosity, which together create the conditions for original ideas.
key_points: In fields like science, investing, startups, and essays, novelty is essential; consensus correctness yields little value.
People often misjudge their own independent-mindedness because schools don’t measure it and social feedback is skewed.
Independent-mindedness can be strengthened by limiting exposure to conventional beliefs, surrounding yourself with independent thinkers, and diversifying your peer groups.
Fastidiousness about truth, resistance to being told what to think, and curiosity form the core components that can substitute for one another.
Curiosity is the most practical lever: seek topics that engage it and indulge it to compound into more questions and ideas.
money_quotes: There are some kinds of work that you can't do well without thinking differently from your peers.
The only valuable insights are the ones most other investors don't share.
It's hard to be a conformist if you don't know what you're supposed to conform to.
The best place to find undiscovered ideas is where no one else is looking.
Perhaps, if your goal is to discover novel ideas, your motto should not be "do what you love" so much as "do what you're curious about."
posts.key_insight: Original ideas come from cultivating independent-mindedness—through truthfulness, skepticism, and curiosity—so you can see what conventional thinking obscures.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that YC funds founders who deeply understand users and can make something they want, not those who exploit people. The same qualities that ace YC interviews—authentic user insight, honest answers, and founder resilience—also explain how people build billion-dollar companies. He rejects the political claim that exploitation is the defining path to billionaire status, saying sustained focus on delighting users is.
key_points: YC’s core heuristic is whether founders know users and can make what they want.
Great startups start in “larval markets” and expand to huge markets over time.
Founders who are genuinely interested in their project persist longer and win.
Exploitation is a bad founder trait and tends to fail at scale.
Fast growth comes from relentless focus on users’ needs.
money_quotes: What YC looks for, above all, is founders who understand some group of users and can make what they want.
If I had to decide whether to fund startups based on a single question, it would be "How do you know people want this?"
The best thing you can do in a YC interview is to teach the partners about your users.
That, not exploiting people, is the defining quality of people who become billionaires from starting companies.
It's all about users.
posts.key_insight: Sustainable billionaire outcomes in startups come from authentic, user-obsessed founders who keep building because they care about the problem, not from exploiting people.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that Airbnb’s success came less from the idea and more from the founders’ unusually earnest, relentless execution. Their firsthand experience as hosts gave them conviction to persist through near-failure, and their focus on a high-demand niche plus hands-on improvements triggered the first real growth. Once they reached ramen profitability, they were effectively airborne and unstoppable.
key_points: The founders’ intensity and follow-through were exceptional and immediately evident.
Personal conviction from hosting kept them going despite debt, rejection, and recession.
Getting growth started required focusing on the hottest market segment and doing unscalable, in-person work.
Ramen profitability marked the key survival threshold that removed dependency on investors.
Momentum came once early growth proved real, and they chose not to ease up.
money_quotes: What was special about the Airbnbs was how earnest they were.
No one ever worked harder during YC than the Airbnbs did.
Any normal person would have given up already.
They'd seen a glimpse of the future, and they couldn't let it go.
We are not going to slow down.
posts.key_insight: Airbnb succeeded because unusually driven founders, convinced by firsthand experience, did unscalable work to spark growth and reach ramen profitability, after which momentum carried them forward.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that earnestness—genuine interest in a problem plus maximal effort—is a decisive advantage for founders, especially when paired with capability. Many people pursue startups for money or status, but those motivated by the problem itself work harder, persist longer, and ultimately outperform. He also contends that the growing alignment between intellectual curiosity and making money is a major, underappreciated shift enabled by modern startups.
key_points: Earnestness is about both right motives and strong effort, and it correlates with startup success.
Nerd-like genuine interest is a powerful motivator but can look naive or uncool, especially early on.
Earnestness matters most in fields where real problems dominate over posturing or cynicism.
Startups increasingly align intellectual curiosity with wealth, which accelerates progress.
money_quotes: When you call someone earnest, you're making a statement about their motives.
A genuine interest in something is a very powerful motivator Â for some people, the most powerful motivator of all.
Caring constrains you.
It's much easier now to make a lot of money by working on something you're interested in than it was 50 years ago.
These are two of the most powerful forces in the world, and in my lifetime they've become significantly more aligned.
posts.key_insight: The founders who win are usually the ones driven by genuine curiosity, because earnestness fuels the effort and resilience that markets reward.

posts.summary: Paul Graham traces his shifting pursuits—programming, AI, painting, startups, and essays—and shows how each phase taught him what kinds of work felt real and lasting. He argues that working on unfashionable things for genuine reasons, following curiosity over prestige, and learning through making led to his most meaningful outcomes, from Viaweb and Y Combinator to his essays and Lisp projects. The essay is a reflective account of how a messy path can still cohere into a life of deliberate work.
key_points: Early fascination with programming and Lisp shaped his approach to building and learning.
Disillusionment with AI and academia pushed him toward making things and seeking work that lasts.
Art school and painting taught him about perception, effort, and independence, but also about institutional hollowness.
Viaweb’s hard lessons on startups and growth fed directly into YC’s model and philosophy.
Publishing essays online revealed a new medium and became a core, lasting part of his work.
money_quotes: I wanted not just to build things, but to build things that would last.
It helped to have studied art, because the main goal of an online store builder is to make users look legit, and the key to looking legit is high production values.
Another thing I didn't get at the time is that growth rate is the ultimate test of a startup.
One of the most conspicuous patterns I've noticed in my life is how well it has worked, for me at least, to work on things that weren't prestigious.
Attention is a zero sum game.
posts.key_insight: Follow work that genuinely pulls you—even if it lacks prestige—because that’s where lasting value, learning, and meaningful outcomes tend to emerge.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that restricted donations are usually a bad idea because nonprofits are better positioned than donors to decide how funds should be used. If a nonprofit doesn’t know better, it’s incompetent and not worth supporting. Exceptions exist for umbrella organizations or when donors are genuine domain experts, but otherwise unrestricted giving does more good.
key_points: Restricted donations are inherently suboptimal because they override the nonprofit’s judgment about needs.
If a nonprofit can’t allocate funds better than donors, it’s not competent enough to merit support.
Donors restrict funds for motives beyond impact, including publicity and signaling.
Nonprofits rarely push back because of asymmetric power and financial anxiety.
Unrestricted donations generally create more impact except in a few special cases.
money_quotes: The secret curse of the nonprofit world is restricted donations.
But who has a better understanding of where money needs to be spent, the nonprofit or the donor?
If a nonprofit doesn't understand better than its donors where money needs to be spent, then it's incompetent and you shouldn't be donating to it at all.
Which means a restricted donation is inherently suboptimal.
If you want to donate to a nonprofit, donate unrestricted. If you trust them to spend your money, trust them to decide how.
posts.key_insight: If you trust a nonprofit enough to donate, you should trust it enough to choose how to use the funds—otherwise you shouldn’t donate at all.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that writing with ordinary words and simple sentences makes ideas easier to absorb, keeps readers engaged longer, and is more considerate to a global audience. Simple writing also exposes weak thinking, lasts better over time, and ultimately reflects good craftsmanship. He writes simply because complexity feels clumsy to him, and he achieves it through fast drafting and extensive cutting during revision.
key_points: Lower reading friction lets readers invest more energy in the ideas and finish the piece.
Simple writing is more respectful to readers, including non-native English speakers.
Fancy prose can hide a lack of ideas, while simple prose keeps the writer honest.
Plain style ages better as language and culture change.
He writes simply through rapid drafting followed by days of editing and cutting.
money_quotes: I try to write using ordinary words and simple sentences.
The less energy they expend on your prose, the more they'll have left for your ideas.
When you write in a fancy way to impress people, you're making them do extra work just so you can seem cool.
Whereas writing simply keeps you honest.
Indeed, lasting is not merely an accidental quality of chairs, or writing. It's a sign you did a good job.
posts.key_insight: Clear, simple prose lowers friction, respects readers, and reveals the true strength of your ideas.

posts.summary: The essay argues that debates about the death penalty miss the real, practical issue: the system frequently condemns innocent people. Given the prevalence of wrongful convictions driven by pressure, misconduct, and flawed evidence, capital punishment in practice becomes state-sanctioned killing of innocents.
key_points: Wrongful convictions in murder cases are common, not rare.
Systemic incentives and failures (police, prosecutors, experts, defense) produce false convictions.
Because innocent people are sentenced to death, theoretical debates about morality or deterrence are secondary.
Capital punishment in practice equates to killing innocents.
money_quotes: But in practice the debate about the death penalty is not about whether it's ok to kill murderers. It's about whether it's ok to kill innocent people, because at least 4% of people on death row are innocent.
Far from being rare, wrongful murder convictions are very common.
Police are under pressure to solve a crime that has gotten a lot of attention.
This circus of incompetence and dishonesty is the real issue with the death penalty.
Whatever it means in theory, in practice capital punishment means killing innocent people.
posts.key_insight: The decisive argument against the death penalty is not abstract ethics or deterrence, but the reality that the system routinely condemns and executes innocent people.

posts.summary: Graham argues that the share of inherited wealth among the richest Americans has fallen because far more people now create fortunes by founding companies, especially tech firms. This shift is a return to a historical norm that was suppressed mid‑20th century by consolidation and oligopolies, and it is amplified today by cheaper startup costs and faster company growth. As a result, more founders get very rich very quickly, which helps explain rising inequality without resorting to purely political explanations.
key_points: The dominant source of new fortunes has shifted from inheritance, oil, and real estate to founder equity in tech‑driven companies.
The mid‑20th century corporate oligopoly era was an anomaly that constrained startups; recent decades represent a reversion to earlier patterns of entrepreneurial wealth creation.
Falling startup costs and faster growth rates increase both the number of founders and the value of their companies.
Investors now need founders more than founders need investors, leading to higher valuations and founder ownership.
Rising inequality is a predictable consequence of more people starting more valuable companies.
money_quotes: The reason the percentage of heirs has decreased is not that fewer people are inheriting great fortunes, but that more people are making them.
So it's not 2020 that's the anomaly here, but 1982.
But now startups are punching right through the middle of the ice crust and displacing incumbents like retailers and TV networks and car companies.
It's easier now to start and grow a company than it has ever been.
Of course the Gini coefficient is increasing. With more people starting more valuable companies, how could it not be?
posts.key_insight: Modern riches come increasingly from founding fast‑growing tech‑driven startups, a historical reversion enabled by lower startup costs and faster growth that naturally widens inequality.

posts.summary: Graham argues that some nerds are "fierce": intensely competitive, overconfident, intelligent, independent-minded, and impatient, which makes them unusually effective at solving hard problems and increasingly dominant in business. These traits can drive outsized success but, if not channeled into ambitious work, can curdle into bitterness and antagonism.
key_points: Fierce nerds are more competitive and personally invested in outcomes than typical high-achievers, and their confidence often becomes self-fulfilling.
Their independent-mindedness and impatience push them toward unconventional approaches and rapid problem-solving.
These traits have become especially valuable in modern business, enabling nerds to win in arenas once dominated by charisma and dealmaking.
Unexercised fierceness tends to become bitterness; ambitious projects provide the healthiest outlet.
The broader shift of power toward technical competence creates a lasting tailwind for nerds.
money_quotes: In fact some nerds are quite fierce.
Up to a point, confidence is a self-fullfilling prophecy.
No one who was both independent-minded and ambitious would want to waste the effort it takes to fit in.
The bad news is that if it's not exercised, your fierceness will turn to bitterness, and you will become an intellectual playground bully.
There has never been a better time to be a nerd.
posts.key_insight: Fierce nerd traits are a powerful engine for achievement, but only if directed into ambitious work rather than left to fester into bitterness.

posts.summary: Graham argues that when a reasonable domain expert proposes an idea that sounds crazy, the safest response is curiosity rather than dismissal, because historically major breakthroughs start that way. While most implausible ideas are bad, the subset proposed by credible experts has unusually high expected value due to paradigms filtering them too harshly and social incentives to attack rather than nurture them.
key_points: Implausible ideas from credible experts are disproportionately likely to be interesting and high-impact.
The right response is to ask questions, since either your model or theirs is wrong.
Paradigms bias everyone, making new ideas look weak at birth and raising the bar unfairly.
People dismiss new ideas due to envy, easy signaling of sophistication, vested interests, or factionalism.
Learning how past ideas looked when new helps you recognize and support emerging ones today.
money_quotes: If the person proposing the idea is reasonable, then they know how implausible it sounds.
There's something like an efficient market here: on average the ideas that seem craziest will, if correct, have the biggest effect.
The wise response to such an idea is not to make statements, but to ask questions, because there's a real mystery here.
The main thing that leads reasonable people to dismiss new ideas is the same thing that holds people back from proposing them: the sheer pervasiveness of the current paradigm.
If you're nice, as well as wise, you won't merely resist attacking such people, but encourage them.
posts.key_insight: When a sane expert proposes a crazy-sounding idea, treat it as a high-upside mystery rather than a mistake, because paradigm bias makes true breakthroughs look weakest at birth.

posts.summary: Paul Graham highlights Noora Health’s NFT auction, arguing it is a concrete, measurable way to fund life-saving healthcare. He emphasizes the nonprofit’s proven impact and cost effectiveness, and notes that unlike most NFTs, this one becomes more valuable as its price rises because it saves more lives.
key_points: Noora Health trains new mothers in South Asian hospitals and can quantify its impact.
A large study shows the program saves 9 babies per 1,000 live births.
The cost per life saved is exceptionally low at $1,235 by conservative accounting.
This NFT’s proceeds will be transparently tracked, tying auction price to lives saved.
money_quotes: For every 1000 live births, they save 9 babies.
The cost of saving a life is the lowest I've seen. $1,235.
This NFT gets better as the price gets higher.
But the higher the price of this NFT goes, the more lives will be saved.
What a sentence to be able to write.
posts.key_insight: This NFT is compelling because its rising price directly translates into more lives saved, making its value both financial and humanitarian.

posts.summary: Graham argues that doing great work requires the rare combination of natural ability, extensive practice, and sustained effort, and that there is no shortcut around hard work. He emphasizes that real work has a distinct, sometimes hard-to-define shape, and that progress depends on constant honesty about effort, results, and what problems are truly worth pursuing.
key_points: Great work requires the combination of ability, practice, and effort; lacking any one limits outcomes.
Real work often differs from school or busywork, and recognizing it is essential to loving and sustaining it.
Working hard includes calibrating hours and intensity to maintain quality, not just maximizing time.
Choosing what to work on is ongoing; interest is the best test, but only if you are honest about motives.
Honesty with yourself is the core mechanism that keeps the whole system aligned and productive.
money_quotes: There are three ingredients in great work: natural ability, practice, and effort.
But you'll need both if you want to be an outlier yourself.
The best test of whether it's worthwhile to work on something is whether you find it interesting.
Working hard is not just a dial you turn up to 11.
But if you're consistently honest and clear-sighted, it will automatically assume an optimal shape, and you'll be productive in a way few people are.
posts.key_insight: Lasting, exceptional work comes from aligning honest self-knowledge about effort, quality, and interests with sustained hard work on the most important problems.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that the most valuable work happens when people are “skating”: deeply engaged in projects they own, which feels more alive and produces better results than dutiful, assigned work. School and adult norms often push people away from this mode, but autonomy and clean-sheet projects preserve it, especially in programming and startups. The key is to recapture the childlike confidence to start projects while being deliberate about choosing work that feels like your own.
key_points: Projects of one’s own create a distinct, highly productive mode of work that feels more alive than routine tasks.
School tends to separate “real work” from personal projects, weakening the path from childhood curiosity to adult excellence.
Ownership has two dimensions: voluntary choice and personal autonomy, both of which affect motivation.
Startups and clean-sheet engineering amplify productivity by attracting and sustaining “skaters.”
High adult standards often block the crucial first step of starting; recapturing childlike confidence helps.
money_quotes: Working on a project of your own is as different from ordinary work as skating is from walking.
You feel as if you're an animal in its natural habitat, doing what you were meant to do — not always happy, maybe, but awake and alive.

It's a bit sad to think of all the high school kids turning their backs on building treehouses and sitting in class dutifully learning about Darwin or Newton to pass some exam, when the work that made Darwin and Newton famous was actually closer in spirit to building treehouses than studying for exams.

People who've never experienced the thrill of working on a project they're excited about can't distinguish this kind of working long hours from the kind that happens in sweatshops and boiler rooms, but they're at opposite ends of the spectrum.
The most important phase in a project of one's own is at the beginning: when you go from thinking it might be cool to do x to actually doing x.
posts.key_insight: The highest-quality work comes from projects you truly own, and the hardest but most important step is simply starting them with the confidence you once had as a kid.

posts.summary: Graham argues that most programming is just gluing library calls, which makes popular languages seem equivalent to many developers. The truly interesting part of programming lies outside that intersection, and the “weirdness” of certain languages signals new ways of thinking and programming that reveal what’s possible beyond glue code.
key_points: Most programmers judge languages based on glue programming, where popular languages are similar.
Weird languages are intentionally strange because they enable different, more powerful techniques.
Lisp macros exemplify program-manipulating approaches that go beyond ordinary library composition.
Learning a weird language expands what you can express and even how you think.
money_quotes: When people say that in their experience all programming languages are basically equivalent, they're making a statement not about languages but about the kind of programming they've done.
99.5% of programming consists of gluing together calls to library functions.
The weirdness of the good ones usually implies the existence of some form of programming that's not just the usual gluing together of library calls.
So if you want to expand your concept of what programming can be, one way to do it is by learning weird languages.
In the process of learning how to say things you couldn't previously say, you'll probably be learning how to think things you couldn't previously think.
posts.key_insight: Exploring intentionally weird languages reveals programming techniques and ways of thinking that ordinary glue-code work in popular languages can’t teach you.

posts.summary: Graham argues that what made Einstein special wasn’t sheer intelligence but the generation of important new ideas, and the two are not identical. Intelligence is necessary but insufficient; many other ingredients—some cultivable—drive discovery, and focusing on them makes the problem of doing great work both more hopeful and more complex.
key_points: Intelligence is a prerequisite for new ideas but doesn’t guarantee them.
Society overvalues measurable intelligence and undervalues idea-generation.
Key ingredients in discovery include obsessive interest, independent-mindedness, learned techniques, and mundane factors like sleep and colleagues.
Writing can be a form of thinking that enables certain kinds of new ideas.
Exploring the gap between intelligence and discovery is fertile ground for learning how discovery happens.
money_quotes: What was special about him was that he had important new ideas.
Being very smart was a necessary precondition for having those ideas, but the two are not identical.
Imagine you had a choice between being really smart but discovering nothing new, and being less smart but discovering lots of new ideas.
There is a kind of thinking that one does by writing, and if you're clumsy at writing, or don't enjoy doing it, that will get in your way if you try to do this kind of thinking.
If we flip the question, and start inquiring into the other ingredients in new ideas that it implies must exist, we can mine this gap for discoveries about discovery.
posts.key_insight: The real lever for doing great work isn’t just being smart but cultivating the other, learnable ingredients that turn intelligence into new ideas.

posts.summary: Graham argues that good taste does exist, and he defends this by showing that denying it leads to absurd conclusions like the impossibility of good art or skilled artists. He explains that while taste is not perfectly objective, human responses are not random, so works can be better or worse at affecting people. Good taste is therefore real but partial, with room for disagreement and improvement.
key_points: Denying good taste implies denying good art and the possibility of artistic skill.
Experience and practice (like painting) reveal clear differences in quality that are not imaginary.
Disagreement about art often stems from extraneous influences rather than the absence of real standards.
Objectivity in taste is a matter of degree because humans share enough common responses to support judgments.
Good taste is possible though not perfect; it forms a partial order rather than a total ranking.
money_quotes: If we start from the premise that there's no such thing as good taste, we end up with conclusions that are obviously false, and therefore the premise must be wrong.
So if you want to discard the concept of good taste, you also have to discard the concept of good art.
It means we can't say that any painter is better than a randomly chosen eight year old.
The distinction between "objective" and "subjective" is not binary, but a matter of degree, depending on how much the subjects have in common.
So while it's not possible to have perfect taste, it is possible to have good taste.
posts.key_insight: Good taste is real but imperfect because shared human responses allow meaningful judgments of quality even amid disagreement.

posts.summary: Graham argues that writing is a severe test that forces ideas to become precise and complete, revealing gaps and generating new insights during the act of composition. Because ideas feel complete until you try to express them, anyone who hasn’t written about a topic hasn’t fully formed their thoughts on it. Writing isn’t sufficient for correctness, but it’s necessary for fully formed ideas.
key_points: Writing exposes imprecision and incompleteness, often producing new ideas during the process.
Reading your work as a neutral outsider is the real test; it forces revisions that trade elegance for accuracy.
Fully formed ideas about nontrivial topics require writing; without it, you don’t realize what’s missing.
money_quotes: Writing about something, even something you know well, usually shows you that you didn't know it as well as you thought.
Putting ideas into words is a severe test.
Half the ideas that end up in an essay will be ones you thought of while you were writing it.
If writing down your ideas always makes them more precise and more complete, then no one who hasn't written about a topic has fully formed ideas about it.
Putting ideas into words is certainly no guarantee that they'll be right. Far from it. But though it's not a sufficient condition, it is a necessary one.
posts.key_insight: Writing is the necessary discipline that turns vague, incomplete thoughts into fully formed ideas.

posts.summary: Graham argues that a modern, secular form of heresy has re-emerged, where certain opinions are punished regardless of their truth and outweigh a person’s other actions. He explains this as a wave of intolerance driven by aggressively conventional-minded people unified by a quasi-religious ideology, and contends that while the trend has narrowed free expression recently, it can be pushed back. He ends on cautious optimism that the current wave is peaking and that societies can develop better defenses against future cycles.
key_points: Heresy functions as opinion-policing that overrides truth and eclipses a person’s other merits.
Labels like “x-ist” are used to end discussion rather than evaluate truth, and their application depends on who speaks.
Waves of intolerance require both intolerant people and a unifying ideology with purity rules.
Recent academic and social media dynamics have amplified a left-originating wave, but future waves could come from the right.
Despite recent contraction, the long-term trend in free expression is positive and can recover with pushback.
money_quotes: Structurally there are two distinctive things about heresy: (1) that it takes priority over the question of truth or falsity, and (2) that it outweighs everything else the speaker has done.
Using such labels is the conversational equivalent of signalling an exception.
A heresy is an opinion whose expression is treated like a crime — one that makes some people feel not merely that you're mistaken, but that you should be punished.
You need two ingredients for a wave of intolerance: intolerant people, and an ideology to guide them.
Overall I'm optimistic.
posts.key_insight: Modern heresy is less about truth than social punishment, and resisting it requires weakening the very concept of heresy so free expression can keep expanding.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that YC’s core value comes from repeated exposure to early-stage startups: most problems recur, but solving them still requires individualized advice and founder focus. Founders often misdiagnose or misprioritize problems, resist counterintuitive guidance, and need rapid, measured iteration. Beyond advice, YC’s unique power is clustering ambitious founders so they can learn, move faster, and help one another.
key_points: Startup problems are surprisingly repetitive across companies, but effective advice must be tailored to each founder.
Founders frequently misidentify or misprioritize their real problems, and often ignore advice because it feels counterintuitive.
YC’s main operational value is sharpening focus so founders can move faster through short feedback loops.
Founders must unlearn “hacking the test” and instead do the real work startups require.
The founder community itself is a major advantage: peers provide energy, perspective, and generous support.
money_quotes: Speed defines startups. Focus enables speed. YC improves focus.
Often founders know what their problems are, but not their relative importance.
The reason founders don't listen to us is that they don't believe us.

A small improvement in navigational ability can make you a lot faster, because it has a double effect: the path is shorter, and you can travel faster along it when you're more certain it's the right one.
However good you are, good colleagues make you better.
posts.key_insight: YC’s leverage comes from diagnosing the right problem, enforcing focus, and surrounding founders with peers so they can iterate quickly toward what actually works.

posts.summary: Graham argues that beyond math and physics, there are general truths that any intelligent beings would share, and we can use “alien truth” as a heuristic target for finding them. He suggests this search effectively defines the core of philosophy: discovering broadly universal ideas without needing perfect certainty about their universality.
key_points: Alien truth serves as a threshold for the most general truths short of math and physics.
Heuristics like controlled experiments, practice improves skill, and Occam’s razor likely qualify as alien truths.
Using alien truth as a generous heuristic is more useful than requiring certainty.
The idea aligns with Erdos’s “God’s book,” extending universality beyond math.
Philosophy’s core task may be the search for alien truths, regardless of what it is called.
money_quotes: If you're trying to find the most general truths short of those of math or physics, then presumably they'll be those we'd share in common with other forms of intelligent life.
Alien truth will work best as a heuristic if we err on the side of generosity.
If there's such a thing as alien truth, then there's more in God's book than math.
One could even make the case that the search for alien truth is, if not an accurate description of philosophy, a good definition for it.
Whatever we call it, the attempt to discover alien truths would be a worthwhile undertaking.
posts.key_insight: Using the idea of truths shared by any intelligence as a target helps identify the most universal human ideas and may capture the true aim of philosophy.

posts.summary: Graham argues that reading can’t be replaced by faster knowledge transfer because reading also teaches writing, and writing is essential for having ideas. Writing is a form of thinking that enables discovery, especially for complex, ill-defined problems, so those who don’t read and write well are at a disadvantage.
key_points: Reading is irreplaceable because it teaches writing, not just information.
Writing is a way to develop and discover ideas, not merely record them.
Complex, ill-defined problems are best solved by writing about them.
