You can’t think well without writing well, and you can’t write well without reading well.
People who want ideas must read, not just seek information.
money_quotes: Reading about x doesn't just teach you about x; it also teaches you how to write.
Writing is not just a way to convey ideas, but also a way to have them.
A good writer will almost always discover new things in the process of writing.
There is a kind of thinking that can only be done by writing.
You can't think well without writing well, and you can't write well without reading well.
posts.key_insight: Reading well is indispensable because it trains writing, and writing is the unique form of thinking needed to develop and discover ideas.

posts.summary: Graham explores the tension between feeling free to choose and being a deterministic machine, testing the claim that you can do what you want but cannot want what you want. He argues that people can sometimes change their desires, but there is always some deeper level of desire that remains beyond control. The correct statement is that you can do what you want, yet at some higher-order level you can’t change the want that ultimately drives you.
key_points: The simple claim that you can’t want what you want is too strong because people can alter some desires.
Higher-order desires can also change, but this becomes increasingly rare at deeper levels.
There is always a level of desire you don’t control, which reconciles agency with mechanistic behavior.
Randomness doesn’t solve the puzzle because it removes responsibility as effectively as determinism.
money_quotes: You can do what you want, but you can't want what you want.
People who don't want to want something — drug addicts, for example — can sometimes make themselves stop wanting it.
It's rare for people to change what they want to want, and the more "want to"s we add, the rarer it gets.
You can do what you want, but there's some statement of the form "you can't (want to)* want what you want" that's true.
Ultimately you get back to a want that you don't control.
posts.key_insight: We can reshape some of our desires, but at some higher-order level there is always a want we do not control, which limits ultimate freedom while preserving everyday agency.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that new ideas come from noticing anomalies—things that seem strange, missing, or broken. The richest source of these anomalies is at the frontiers of knowledge, where close inspection reveals obvious gaps. Exploring such gaps can lead to entirely new directions of understanding.
key_points: Notice anomalies as the primary method for generating new ideas.
Frontiers of knowledge reveal the most fertile gaps for exploration.
Knowledge expands fractally, hiding many unseen gaps up close.
Obvious-seeming gaps can unlock new branches of insight.
money_quotes: The way to get new ideas is to notice anomalies: what seems strange, or missing, or broken?
You can see anomalies in everyday life (much of standup comedy is based on this), but the best place to look for them is at the frontiers of knowledge.
Knowledge grows fractally.
From a distance its edges look smooth, but when you learn enough to get close to one, you'll notice it's full of gaps.
In the best case, exploring such gaps yields whole new fractal buds.
posts.key_insight: New ideas emerge by looking closely at the frontiers of knowledge, where apparently obvious gaps reveal themselves as opportunities for discovery.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that great work comes from choosing a field that matches your aptitude and deep curiosity, then pushing to the frontier, noticing gaps, and exploring them with sustained effort. He emphasizes curiosity, earnestness, and independent-mindedness over rigid planning, and says consistency and risk-taking compound into breakthrough results. The essay also frames morale, audience, and environment as practical levers that keep ambitious projects moving.
key_points: Pick work that combines aptitude and intense interest, then learn enough to reach the frontier and spot gaps.
Curiosity is the engine of discovery and should guide what you work on and how you persist.
Great work favors earnestness, originality, and willingness to break rules over affectation and fashion.
Start small, iterate in successive versions, and avoid overplanning in flexible domains.
Protect morale and focus, because consistency and compounding effort matter more than spurts.
money_quotes: The work you choose needs to have three qualities: it has to be something you have a natural aptitude for, that you have a deep interest in, and that offers scope to do great work.
Curiosity is the best guide.
Great things are almost always made in successive versions.
Original ideas don't come from trying to have original ideas.
If you asked an oracle the secret to doing great work and the oracle replied with a single word, my bet would be on "curiosity."
posts.key_insight: Great work is most likely when you let deep curiosity guide your choice of problems and then persist with earnest, iterative effort to explore gaps at the frontier.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that returns for performance are often superlinear, driven by exponential growth and thresholds, so small differences in performance can lead to outsized outcomes. He claims this pattern is fundamental across business, learning, fame, and discovery, and that understanding it is crucial for ambitious people. He also suggests the modern decline of institutional gatekeeping is expanding opportunities for superlinear returns, with higher inequality as a consequence.
key_points: Superlinear returns are a fundamental feature of the world, not an artifact of rules, and appear in business, fame, power, and knowledge.
Two main causes drive superlinear returns: exponential growth and thresholds; many situations combine both.
Learning compounds and is the most reliable path to superlinear returns; always be learning and follow curiosity.
The erosion of institutional gatekeeping increases variance in outcomes, creating more opportunity and more inequality.
To benefit, do exceptionally good work, pick problems that compound, and take risks early to get multiple shots.
money_quotes: You can't understand the world without understanding the concept of superlinear returns.
Whenever how well you do depends on how well you've done, you'll get exponential growth.
If you're not learning, you're probably not on a path that leads to superlinear returns.
Superlinear returns imply inequality. The steeper the return curve, the greater the variation in outcomes.
When in doubt, follow your curiosity. It never lies, and it knows more than you do about what's worth paying attention to.
posts.key_insight: Superlinear returns arise from compounding and thresholds, so the best strategy is to pursue work that accelerates learning and growth, even at the cost of higher risk and inequality.

posts.summary: Graham asks what the “best essay” would be like and concludes that, by his own criteria, it would usually be an essay that reveals a major new discovery. That reframes the problem into how to write essays well: start with a compelling question, use writing to discover ideas, and iteratively refine by strict rereading and branching toward novelty and generality. He argues that great essays depend on the quality of questions and the writer’s curiosity, breadth, and willingness to revise, with timelessness emerging only in special cases.
key_points: The best possible essay at a given time would usually present the most important new discovery available.
Writing essays well is a method of discovery: start with a question, commit to words, and refine by ruthless rereading.
Novelty and generality guide which branches of thought to pursue; rewriting allows backtracking.
Great initial questions often feel outrageous, imply breadth, and align with genuine curiosity.
Timeless essays are rare because effective ideas get absorbed into culture; breadth is the deeper aim.
money_quotes: The best essay would be on the most important topic you could tell people something surprising about.
Writing essays, at its best, is a way of discovering ideas.
Writing converts your ideas from vague to bad.
One of the most dangerous temptations in writing (and in software and painting) is to keep something that isn't right, just because it contains a few good bits or cost you a lot of effort.
If you want to surprise readers not just now but in the future as well, you have to write essays that won't stick.
posts.key_insight: Great essays come from using writing as a discovery process, where the quality of the initial questions—and relentless refinement toward novelty and generality—determines how far the ideas can go.

posts.summary: Graham argues that the best way to set yourself up to start a Google-scale startup is to get good at some technology by building your own projects, because that both builds skill and naturally reveals valuable ideas. The right ideas usually emerge from solving your own and your friends' problems, and cofounders are best found by working together on projects, often at strong universities.
key_points: Work on your own projects to build real technological skill; classes and credentials are insufficient signals.
Choose technologies based on genuine interest, not predictions about what will be hot.
Startup ideas come from noticing missing or broken things once you’re skilled; projects are the best source.
Build things you and your friends truly want; that demand is the seed of a good startup.
Do well in school to access strong universities, which concentrate potential cofounders and early hires.
money_quotes: The trick is to start your own company.
If you're not sure what technology to get good at, get good at programming.
Once you're good at some technology, when you look at the world you see dotted outlines around the things that are missing.
That's why the best startups grow out of projects that aren't meant to be startups.
That's it, just two things, build stuff and do well in school.
posts.key_insight: Build things you care about to gain real skill and uncover natural startup ideas, then surround yourself with strong collaborators to turn those projects into companies.

posts.summary: Paul Graham recounts how meeting Steve Huffman and Alexis Ohanian before YC led him to start the program and ultimately push them to pivot from a bad idea to what became Reddit. He argues that Reddit’s success was tightly coupled to its founders’ personalities and curiosity, and that a simple, fast launch plus user-driven freshness made the site unusually resilient even through years of weak management. The piece closes by noting how Reddit’s potential surged once Steve returned, showing the power of founder fit and enduring product utility.
key_points: Graham met the founders before YC existed and was inspired enough to start YC largely to fund them.
Their original idea was unworkable, but he pushed them to pivot to a link-sharing site modeled on del.icio.us/popular.
Reddit’s personality matched Steve Huffman’s curiosity and skepticism, shaping the product’s ethos.
User-submitted content made Reddit fresher than moderated competitors like Slashdot.
Reddit’s core utility made it “unkillable,” and its growth accelerated once founder leadership returned.
money_quotes: There needed to be something like del.icio.us/popular, but designed for sharing links instead of being a byproduct of saving them.
As with all the really great startups, there's an uncannily close match between the company and the founders.
Reddit has a certain personality — curious, skeptical, ready to be amused — and that personality is Steve's.
Being driven by user submissions meant Reddit was fresher than Slashdot. News there was newer, and users will always go where the newest news is.
Reddit the site (and now app) is such a fundamentally useful thing that it's almost unkillable.
posts.key_insight: Founders who fit a product’s core personality, combined with a fast, simple launch, can create a utility so fundamental that it endures even through years of poor management.

posts.summary: Graham argues that persistence and obstinacy are different behaviors, not just labels applied after the fact. The persistent stay committed to a goal while remaining flexible on methods, listening to criticism and updating their plans. Obstinacy is a reflexive resistance to changing ideas, which can work for simple problems but fails on hard ones.
key_points: Persistent people are goal-attached and method-flexible; obstinate people cling to their initial ideas.
The persistent actively seek disagreement to find flaws, while the obstinate tune it out.
Obstinacy is a simple, primitive reflex that can prevent panic but breaks down on complex problems.
Persistence is built from five qualities: energy, imagination, resilience, judgment, and goal focus.
Success on hard problems requires changing one’s mind as evidence accumulates.
money_quotes: The persistent are attached to the goal. The obstinate are attached to their ideas about how to reach it.
The reason the persistent and the obstinate seem similar is that they're both hard to stop.
Resilience means setbacks can't change your morale, not that they can't change your mind.
Persistence often requires that one change one's mind.

Five distinct qualities — energy, imagination, resilience, good judgement, and focus on a goal — combine to produce a phenomenon that seems a bit like obstinacy in the sense that it causes you not to give up.
posts.key_insight: The right kind of stubbornness is goal-driven persistence that stays open to evidence and keeps adapting its methods.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that the standard management advice for scaling companies fails founders because it describes how professional managers run companies, not how founders should. He posits a distinct “founder mode” that preserves deeper founder involvement, challenges modular delegation, and better fits how successful founders actually lead as companies grow.
key_points: Conventional “hire good people and give them room” advice often backfires for founders scaling startups.
Founder mode differs fundamentally from manager mode and can feel necessary, not optional, to founders.
Founder mode likely involves direct engagement beyond strict reporting lines, such as skip-level contact.
The boundaries of delegation in founder mode are fluid and evolve with trust and company stage.
We lack formal knowledge of founder mode, but early examples suggest it outperforms manager mode.
money_quotes: In effect there are two different ways to run a company: founder mode and manager mode.

Hire good people and give them room to do their jobs. Sounds great when it's described that way, doesn't it? Except in practice, judging from the report of founder after founder, what this often turns out to mean is: hire professional fakers and let them drive the company into the ground.
Whatever founder mode consists of, it's pretty clear that it's going to break the principle that the CEO should engage with the company only via his or her direct reports.
Founder mode will be more complicated than manager mode. But it will also work better.
Imagine what they'll do once we can tell them how to run their companies like Steve Jobs instead of John Sculley.
posts.key_insight: Scaling doesn’t require founders to become managers; it requires a distinct founder-led operating mode that keeps direct, hands-on influence while selectively delegating.

posts.summary: Graham argues that whether to follow your passion depends on your goals and context, not a simple yes/no. If you want moderate money, interest often conflicts with pay, but for massive wealth via startups, working on what fascinates you is a powerful way to discover outlier ideas. Under uncertainty, you should explore interests early, choose “upwind” options, and recognize that great work requires genuine curiosity.
key_points: Most “follow your passion” advice ignores the tradeoff between money and interest except in rare overlaps or in startups aimed at huge outcomes.
Outlier startup ideas often emerge from founders’ genuine curiosities, not from consciously chasing money.
Indecision usually stems from ignorance about oneself, the work, or one’s ability—so the remedy is exploration.
Your colleagues shape you; choosing work for money can yield a culture that feels more hollow over time.
If you want to do great work, working on what genuinely interests you is necessary.
money_quotes: So there's something like a midwit peak for making money.
When you can't decide which path to take, it's almost always due to ignorance.
What do you do in the face of uncertainty? Get more certainty.
One useful trick for judging different kinds of work is to look at who your colleagues will be.
If you do want to [do great work], the complicated question of whether or not to work on what interests you the most becomes simple.
posts.key_insight: Chasing what genuinely interests you is risky for moderate gains but is essential for great work and often the best path to truly outlier success.

posts.summary: Graham argues that AI will remove the practical pressure to learn writing, collapsing the middle tier of writers into a divide between those who can write and those who cannot. Because writing is a form of thinking, this shift risks creating a world of thinkers and think-nots, where clear thought becomes a choice rather than a default expectation.
key_points: Writing is inherently difficult because it requires clear thinking.
Work and status still demand writing, but AI now provides an easy escape from that pressure.
The result will be a divide between people who can write and people who cannot.
Losing writing skill is worse than other obsolete skills because it erodes a key mode of thinking.
Like physical strength today, writing ability will remain only for those who choose to develop it.
money_quotes: The reason so many people have trouble writing is that it's fundamentally difficult.
To write well you have to think clearly, and thinking clearly is hard.
Not anymore. AI has blown this world open.
If you're thinking without writing, you only think you're thinking.
There will still be smart people, but only those who choose to be.
posts.key_insight: If AI removes the need to write, it also removes a primary path to clear thinking, turning intelligence into an opt-in skill.

posts.summary: The essay argues that wokeness is a modern instance of an old pattern: priggish, performative moral enforcement that substitutes orthodoxy for virtue. Paul Graham traces its rise to late-1980s academia, its resurgence in the 2010s via social media, polarizing press incentives, and institutional bureaucracies, and claims it has begun to recede after peaking around 2020. He proposes treating wokeness like a religion in organizations—allowing belief but resisting enforcement—and building cultural antibodies against new heresies to prevent future outbreaks.
key_points: Wokeness is defined as aggressively performative social-justice enforcement rather than a commitment to justice itself.
Political correctness began in late-1980s humanities/social sciences as 1960s radicals gained institutional power.
Social media outrage dynamics and polarized journalism amplified the second wave into a broader societal phenomenon.
Professionalized enforcement (DEI bureaucracy) entrenched wokeness by institutionalizing incentives to find improprieties.
The antidote is pluralism: treat wokeness as a religion in organizations and resist new heresy-creation with a burden of proof for claimed harms.
money_quotes: They both have the same definition: An aggressively performative focus on social justice.
The problem with political correctness was not that it focused on marginalized groups, but the shallow, aggressive way in which it did so.
Whenever a society has a concept of heresy and orthodoxy, orthodoxy becomes a substitute for virtue.
This new class of bureaucrats pursued a woke agenda as if their jobs depended on it, because they did.
The number of true things we can't say should not increase. If it does, something is wrong.
posts.key_insight: Wokeness is a recurring pattern of performative moral enforcement that thrives on new heresies, and the best defense is pluralism plus a default skepticism toward expanding speech taboos.

posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that beyond the obvious duties of helping people and caring for the world, a core principle of how to live well is to make good new things. He claims this captures the highest form of thinking and has become newly central because modern life gives many people the freedom to choose original work, unlike earlier eras focused on prescribed roles.
key_points: Making good new things is a higher-aspiration “should” distinct from moral duty but essential to living up to one’s potential.
Creation is favored over criticism because first steps in making are rarer and more valuable than sophisticated commentary.
Newness is essential in both science and art; repetition, even skillful, is less impressive than original work.
Traditional moral prescriptions answered how to be, not what to do, because most people had little choice in work.
If you can make something amazing, you should do it, even without intending practical benefit, since impact often follows.
money_quotes: The most impressive thing humans can do is to think.
The best kind of thinking, or more precisely the best proof that one has thought well, is to make good new things.
Criticism seems sophisticated, and making new things often seems awkward, especially at first; and yet it's precisely those first steps that are most rare and valuable.

If you're excited about some kind of work that's not considered prestigious and you can explain what everyone else is overlooking about it, then this is not merely a kind of work that's ok to do, but one to seek out.
So if you think you can make something amazing, you should probably just go ahead and do it.
posts.key_insight: To live fully, aim beyond duty by creating genuinely new, good things, because that is both the highest proof of thought and a path to unintended but real benefit.

posts.summary: Graham argues that good-sounding writing and correct ideas are tightly linked rather than independent. Revising for rhythm and flow forces ideas to become more coherent, both by shaking loose better formulations and by making errors easier for the writer-as-first-reader to notice. This connection mainly holds when writing is used to develop ideas, not merely to report them.
key_points: Improving the sound of sentences often improves the correctness and development of ideas.
Rewriting under small constraints tends to yield better prose and clearer thinking, like shaking a bin to pack objects tighter.
Flowing prose makes mistakes more visible because the writer rereads constantly.
Good writing’s rhythm mirrors the natural shape of thought, so fixing sound helps fix meaning.
Beautiful writing can be false if built on false premises; it is more reliably internally consistent than true.
money_quotes: Fixing sentences that sound bad seems to help get the ideas right.
Because the writer is the first reader.
An essay is a cleaned up train of thought, in the same way dialogue is cleaned up conversation, and a train of thought has a natural rhythm.
So it's not quite right to say that better sounding writing is more likely to be true.
It's hard to be right without sounding right.
posts.key_insight: When you’re using writing to think, polishing the sound of the prose is not cosmetic—it’s a reliable way to refine and correct the ideas themselves.

posts.summary: Graham argues that essays teach readers something they don’t already know, and there are three reasons for that ignorance: the topic is unimportant, the reader is obtuse, or the reader is inexperienced. For smart readers on important topics, the highest impact tends to be on younger readers because there is more room to change their thinking. He concludes that understanding this “field” shouldn’t change his approach—he writes to surprise himself by following curiosity.
key_points: Ignorance about a topic stems from it being unimportant, the reader being obtuse, or the reader being inexperienced.
Writing for smart people about important topics naturally targets the young, because they have the most room for surprise.
Essay impact is a tradeoff between how much you change thinking and how important the topic is, with younger readers shifting the balance.
The “essay field” is like a gravity that shapes what writers can do, whether or not they recognize it.
Graham writes to surprise himself, not to optimize for a particular audience age.
money_quotes: If you're writing for smart people about important things, you're writing for the young.
The impact of an essay is how much it changes readers' thinking multiplied by the importance of the topic.
It's hard to have big new ideas about important topics.
The tradeoff isn't a conscious one, at least not for me. It's more like a kind of gravitational field that writers work in.
I'm not trying to surprise readers of any particular age; I'm trying to surprise myself.
posts.key_insight: Writing for smart readers on important topics naturally has the greatest effect on the young because they have the most room for surprise.

posts.summary: Graham argues that programming languages can be compared by the specific shortcomings they aim to fix in prior languages, and that this lens surprisingly fits many languages well. He lists a chain of examples showing each new language as a response to perceived limits, awkwardness, or missing features in earlier ones.
key_points: A useful way to compare languages is by the problem each tries to fix.
Many languages are succinctly explained as reactions to flaws in their predecessors.
The history of languages reads like a sequence of targeted fixes rather than purely novel inventions.
money_quotes: Kevin Kelleher suggested an interesting way to compare programming languages: to describe each in terms of the problem it fixes.
The surprising thing is how many, and how well, languages can be described this way.
Lisp: Turing Machines are an awkward way to describe computation.
Java: C++ is a kludge. And Microsoft is going to crush us.
Prolog: Programming is not enough like logic.
posts.key_insight: Programming languages are often best understood as targeted fixes for the shortcomings of earlier languages.

posts.summary: Graham argues that object-oriented programming is a useful technique in some situations but shouldn't dominate how all programs are written. He claims most reasons for its popularity are weak or context-bound, and prefers composing specific OO features as needed rather than committing to full OO design.
key_points: OO is a tool, not a universal paradigm; forcing everything into classes is unnecessary.
OO often compensates for limitations in languages lacking closures or macros.
Big-company software processes favor OO because it constrains large teams, even at the cost of bloat and duplication.
OO can create the appearance of productivity via extensive scaffolding and ceremony.
Overloading and other OO sub-concepts can be used without committing to full OO systems.
money_quotes: My own feeling is that object-oriented programming is a useful technique in some cases, but it isn't something that has to pervade every program you write.
Object-oriented programming is popular in big companies, because it suits the way they write software.
Object-oriented programming generates a lot of what looks like work.
Overloading, for example, is not intrinsically tied to classes.
But it seems more dangerous to put stuff in that you've never needed because it's thought to be a good idea.
posts.key_insight: Treat object-oriented programming as one of several tools and adopt its specific features only when they genuinely fit the problem.

posts.summary: Graham argues that large programs should be broken into small, comprehensible pieces, but Lisp programmers do this best by working bottom-up as well as top-down. By extending the language with new abstractions, language and program evolve together, yielding code that is smaller, clearer, and more adaptable than a monolithic design.
key_points: Bottom-up design means changing the language to fit the problem, not just decomposing the program top-down.
Building higher-level abstractions reduces program size, component count, and connection complexity.
Reusable utilities accumulate into a substrate that speeds future development.
Abstraction improves readability by shifting understanding from special cases to general operators.
Small teams benefit most from this style, gaining disproportionate productivity.
money_quotes: In Lisp, you don't just write your program down toward the language, you also build the language up toward your program.
Language and program evolve together.
In the end your program will look as if the language had been designed for it.
Instead of a single, monolithic program, you will get a larger language with more abstract operators, and a smaller program written in it.
When a small group also takes advantage of the techniques that Lisp makes possible, it can win outright.
posts.key_insight: By pushing abstractions into the language itself, bottom-up design makes programs smaller, clearer, and more powerful than purely top-down decomposition.
